author: John Selden

quote: "Commonly we say a judgment falls upon a man for something in him we cannot abide."
author: John Selden

quote: "No man is the wiser for his learning."
author: John Selden

quote: "Wit and wisdom are born with a man."
author: John Selden

quote: "Few men make themselves masters of the things they write or speak."
author: John Selden

quote: "Take a straw and throw it up into the air,--you may see by that which way the wind is."
author: John Selden

quote: "They that govern the most make the least noise."
author: John Selden

quote: "Never king dropped out of the clouds."
author: John Selden

quote: "Wise men say nothing in dangerous times."
author: John Selden

quote: "Here are sands, ignoble things, Dropt from the ruined sides of kings."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: On the Tombs of Westminster Abbey

quote: "It is always good When a man has two irons in the fire."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Faithful Friends. Act i. Sc. 2

quote: "But they that are above Have ends in everything."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Maid's Tragedy. Act v. Sc. 1

quote: "There is a method in man's wickedness,-- It grows up by degrees."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: A King and No King. Act v. Sc. 4

quote: "Kiss till the cow comes home."
author: Francis Beaumont

quote: "No better than you should be."
author: Francis Beaumont

quote: "From the crown of the head to the sole of the foot."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Honest Man's Fortune. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "There is no jesting with edge tools."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Little French Lawyer. Act iv. Sc. 7

quote: "Though I say it that should not say it."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: Wit at Several Weapons. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "Whistle, and she'll come to you."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: Wit Without Money. Act iv. Sc. 4

quote: "The fit 's upon me now! Come quickly, gentle lady; The fit 's upon me now."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: Wit Without Money. Act v. Sc. 4

quote: "Of all the paths [that] lead to a woman's love Pity 's the straightest."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Knight of Malta. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "What 's one man's poison, signor, Is another's meat or drink."
author: Francis Beaumont

quote: "Primrose, first-born child of Ver, Merry springtime's harbinger."
author: Francis Beaumont
source: The Two Noble Kinsmen. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "Jack shall pipe and Gill shall dance."
author: George Wither

quote: "Hang sorrow! care will kill a cat, And therefore let 's be merry."
author: George Wither

quote: "Though I am young, I scorn to flit On the wings of borrowed wit."
author: George Wither

quote: "And I oft have heard defended,-- Little said is soonest mended."
author: George Wither

quote: "And he that gives us in these days New Lords may give us new laws."
author: George Wither

quote: "Then fly betimes, for only they Conquer Love that run away."

quote: "Whose life is a bubble, and in length a span."

quote: "Some asked me where the rubies grew, And nothing I did say; But with my finger pointed to The lips of Julia."

source: The Rock of Rubies, and the Quarrie of Pearls

quote: "Some asked how pearls did grow, and where? Then spoke I to my girl To part her lips, and showed them there The quarelets of pearl."
author: Robert Herrick
source: The Rock of Rubies, and the Quarrie of Pearls

quote: "A sweet disorder in the dress Kindles in clothes a wantonness."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Fair daffadills, we weep to see You haste away so soon: As yet the early rising sun Has not attained his noon."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Thus woe succeeds a woe, as wave a wave."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Her pretty feet, like snails, did creep A little out, and then, As if they played at bo-peep, Did soon draw in again."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "I saw a flie within a beade Of amber cleanly buried."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Thus times do shift,--each thing his turn does hold; New things succeed, as former things grow old."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Out-did the meat, out-did the frolick wine."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Attempt the end, and never stand to doubt; Nothing 's so hard but search will find it out."
author: Robert Herrick

quote: "Be wisely worldly, be not worldly wise."
author: Francis Quarles

quote: "The slender debt to Nature 's quickly paid, Discharged, perchance, with greater ease than made."
author: Francis Quarles

quote: "The next way home 's the farthest way about."
author: Francis Quarles

quote: "To write a verse or two is all the praise That I can raise."

quote: "Sweet day, so cool, so calm, so bright, The bridal of the earth and sky."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Sweet spring, full of sweet days and roses, A box where sweets compacted lie."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Like summer friends, Flies of estate and sunneshine."
author: George Herbert

quote: "A verse may find him who a sermon flies, And turn delight into a sacrifice."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Dare to be true: nothing can need a lie; A fault which needs it most, grows two thereby."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Bibles laid open, millions of surprises."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Religion stands on tiptoe in our land, Ready to pass to the American strand."
author: George Herbert

quote: "If goodness lead him not, yet weariness May toss him to my breast."
author: George Herbert

quote: "His bark is worse than his bite."
author: George Herbert

quote: "It is a poor sport that is not worth the candle."
author: George Herbert

quote: "The lion is not so fierce as they paint him."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Words are women, deeds are men."
author: George Herbert

quote: "A dwarf on a giant's shoulders sees farther of the two."
author: George Herbert

quote: "Angling may be said to be so like the mathematics that it can never be fully learnt."

quote: "As no man is born an artist, so no man is born an angler."
author: Izaak Walton

quote: "As the Italians say, Good company in a journey makes the way to seem the shorter."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "I am, sir, a Brother of the Angle."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "It [angling] deserves commendations; . . . it is an art worthy the knowledge and practice of a wise man."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "Angling is somewhat like poetry,--men are to be born so."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "Doubt not but angling will prove to be so pleasant that it will prove to be, like virtue, a reward to itself."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "You will find angling to be like the virtue of humility, which has a calmness of spirit and a world of other blessings attending upon it."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 1

quote: "I remember that a wise friend of mine did usually say, "That which is everybody's business is nobody's business."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. ii

quote: "No man can lose what he never had."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. v

quote: "This dish of meat is too good for any but anglers, or very honest men."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 8

quote: "And upon all that are lovers of virtue, and dare trust in his Providence, and be quiet and go a-angling."
author: Izaak Walton
source: The Complete Angler. Part i. Chap. 21

quote: "The great secretary of Nature,--Sir Francis Bacon."
author: Izaak Walton

quote: "Only the actions of the just Smell sweet and blossom in the dust."
author: James Shirley
source: Contention of Ajax and Ulysses. Sc. 3

quote: "We grant, although he had much wit, He was very shy of using it."

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 45

author: Samuel Butler
source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 81

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 89

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 93

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 121

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 145

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 149

quote: "Such as take lodgings in a head That 's to be let unfurnished."

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 161

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto i. Line 463

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto ii. Line 831

source: Hudibras. Part i. Canto ii. Line 872

source: Hudibras. Part iii. Canto i. Line 481

source: Hudibras. Part iii. Canto i. Line 687

source: Hudibras. Part iii. Canto i. Line 1495

author: Samuel Butler
source: Hudibras. Part iii. Canto iii. Line 547

source: Holy and Profane State. The Good Advocate

quote: "To know That which before us lies in daily life Is the prime wisdom."

quote: "Those graceful acts, Those thousand decencies that daily flow From all her words and actions."

quote: "Revenge, at first though sweet, Bitter ere long back on itself recoils."

quote: "As one who long in populous city pent, Where houses thick and sewers annoy the air."

quote: "Hope elevates, and joy Brightens his crest."

quote: "The olive grove of Academe, Plato's retirement, where the Attic bird Trills her thick-warbled notes the summer long."

quote: "The nodding horror of whose shady brows Threats the forlorn and wandering passenger."

quote: "The star that bids the shepherd fold."

quote: "This cordial julep here, That flames and dances in his crystal bounds."

quote: "Ladies, whose bright eyes Rain influence, and judge the prize."

quote: "The gay motes that people the sunbeams."
author: John Milton

quote: "Sweet bird, that shun'st the noise of folly, Most musical, most melancholy!"

quote: "Where glowing embers through the room Teach light to counterfeit a gloom."

quote: "Far from all resort of mirth Save the cricket on the hearth."

quote: "Where more is meant than meets the ear."
author: John Milton

quote: "Hide me from day's garish eye."

quote: "Till old experience do attain To something like prophetic strain."

quote: "Under the shady roof Of branching elm star-proof."

quote: "She is pretty to walk with, And witty to talk with, And pleasant, too, to think on."
author: Sir John Suckling

quote: "Her face is like the milky way i' the sky,-- A meeting of gentle lights without a name."
author: Sir John Suckling

quote: "But as when an authentic watch is shown, Each man winds up and rectifies his own, So in our very judgments."
author: Sir John Suckling

quote: "The prince of darkness is a gentleman."
author: Sir John Suckling

quote: "He either fears his fate too much, Or his deserts are small, That dares not put it to the touch To gain or lose it all."
author: Marquis of Montrose

quote: "Actions of the last age are like almanacs of the last year."
author: Sir John Denham

quote: "When flowing cups pass swiftly round With no allaying Thames."
author: Richard Lovelace

quote: "Fishes that tipple in the deep, Know no such liberty."
author: Richard Lovelace

quote: "What shall I do to be forever known, And make the age to come my own?"
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "His time is forever, everywhere his place."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "His _faith_, perhaps, in some nice tenets might Be wrong; his _life_, I 'm sure, was in the right."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "The thirsty earth soaks up the rain, And drinks, and gapes for drink again; The plants suck in the earth, and are With constant drinking fresh and fair."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "Fill all the glasses there, for why Should every creature drink but I? Why, man of morals, tell me why?"
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "Hope, of all ills that men endure, The only cheap and universal cure."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "An harmless flaming meteor shone for hair, And fell adown his shoulders with loose care."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "The monster London laugh at me."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "The fairest garden in her looks, And in her mind the wisest books."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "Words that weep and tears that speak."
author: Abraham Cowley

quote: "Thus would I double my life's fading space; For he that runs it well, runs twice his race."
author: Abraham Cowley
source: Discourse xi. Of Myself. St. xi

quote: "Orange bright, Like golden lamps in a green night."
author: Andrew Marvell

quote: "And all the way, to guide their chime, With falling oars they kept the time."
author: Andrew Marvell

quote: "He nothing common did, or mean, Upon that memorable scene."
author: Andrew Marvell

quote: "To make a bank was a great plot of state; Invent a shovel, and be a magistrate."
author: Andrew Marvell

quote: "Liars ought to have good memories."
author: Algernon Sidney
source: Discourses on Government. Chap. ii. Sect. xv

quote: "Men lived like fishes; the great ones devoured the small."
author: Algernon Sidney
source: Discourses on Government. Chap. ii. Sect. xviii

quote: "It is not necessary to light a candle to the sun."
author: Algernon Sidney
source: Discourses on Government. Chap. ii. Sect. xxiii

quote: "Learn to read slow: all other graces Will follow in their proper places."
author: William Walker

quote: "And so I penned It down, until at last it came to be, For length and breadth, the bigness which you see."
author: John Bunyan
source: Pilgrim's Progress. Apology for his Book

quote: "Some said, "John, print it;" others said, "Not so." Some said, "It might do good;" others said, "No."
author: John Bunyan
source: Pilgrim's Progress. Apology for his Book

quote: "The name of the slough was Despond."
author: John Bunyan

quote: "Every fat must stand upon his bottom."
author: John Bunyan

quote: "They came to the Delectable Mountains."
author: John Bunyan

quote: "He that is down needs fear no fall."
author: John Bunyan

quote: "Books, like proverbs, receive their chief value from the stamp and esteem of ages through which they have passed."
author: Sir William Temple

quote: "Whate'er he did was done with so much ease, In him alone 't was natural to please."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 27

quote: "Great wits are sure to madness near allied, And thin partitions do their bounds divide."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 163

quote: "Than a successive title long and dark, Drawn from the mouldy rolls of Noah's ark."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 301

quote: "Not only hating David, but the king."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 512

quote: "Who think too little, and who talk too much."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 534

quote: "Beware the fury of a patient man."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part i. Line 1005

quote: "For every inch that is not fool is rogue."
author: John Dryden
source: Absalom and Achitophel. Part ii. Line 463

quote: "Thus all below is strength, and all above is grace."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Be kind to my remains; and oh defend, Against your judgment, your departed friend!"
author: John Dryden

quote: "Wit will shine Through the harsh cadence of a rugged line."
author: John Dryden
source: To the Memory of Mr. Oldham. Line 15

quote: "Her wit was more than man, her innocence a child."
author: John Dryden

quote: "None but the brave deserves the fair."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Bacchus, ever fair and ever young."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Rich the treasure, Sweet the pleasure,-- Sweet is pleasure after pain."
author: John Dryden

quote: "A very merry, dancing, drinking, Laughing, quaffing, and unthinking time."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Fool, not to know that love endures no tie, And Jove but laughs at lovers' perjury."
author: John Dryden
source: Palamon and Arcite. Book ii. Line 758

quote: "Old as I am, for ladies' love unfit, The power of beauty I remember yet."
author: John Dryden

quote: "The fool of nature stood with stupid eyes And gaping mouth, that testified surprise."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Love taught him shame; and shame, with love at strife, Soon taught the sweet civilities of life."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Of seeming arms to make a short essay, Then hasten to be drunk,--the business of the day."
author: John Dryden

quote: "I can enjoy her while she 's kind; But when she dances in the wind, And shakes the wings and will not stay, I puff the prostitute away."
author: John Dryden
source: Imitation of Horace. Book iii. Ode 29, Line 81

quote: "Ill habits gather by unseen degrees,-- As brooks make rivers, rivers run to seas."
author: John Dryden
source: Ovid, Metamorphoses, Book xv. The Worship of Æsculapius, Line 155

quote: "Look round the habitable world: how few Know their own good, or knowing it, pursue."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Thespis, the first professor of our art, At country wakes sung ballads from a cart."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Errors, like straws, upon the surface flow; He who would search for pearls must dive below."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Men are but children of a larger growth."
author: John Dryden
source: All for Love. Act iv. Sc. 1

quote: "Your ignorance is the mother of your devotion to me."
author: John Dryden
source: The Maiden Queen. Act i. Sc. 2

quote: "I am resolved to grow fat, and look young till forty."
author: John Dryden
source: The Maiden Queen. Act iii. Sc. 1

quote: "What precious drops are those Which silently each other's track pursue, Bright as young diamonds in their infant dew?"
author: John Dryden
source: The Conquest of Granada. Part ii. Act iii. Sc. 1

quote: "Pains of love be sweeter far Than all other pleasures are."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Whatever is, is in its causes just."
author: John Dryden

quote: "His hair just grizzled, As in a green old age."
author: John Dryden

quote: "She, though in full-blown flower of glorious beauty, Grows cold even in the summer of her age."
author: John Dryden

quote: "There is a pleasure sure In being mad which none but madmen know."
author: John Dryden
source: The Spanish Friar. Act ii. Sc. 1

quote: "Bless the hand that gave the blow."
author: John Dryden
source: The Spanish Friar. Act ii. Sc. 1

quote: "Second thoughts, they say, are best."
author: John Dryden
source: The Spanish Friar. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "This is the porcelain clay of humankind."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Whistling to keep myself from being afraid."
author: John Dryden

quote: "Remember Milo's end, Wedged in that timber which he strove to rend."
author: Earl of Roscommon

quote: "Immodest words admit of no defence, For want of decency is want of sense."
author: Earl of Roscommon

quote: "The multitude is always in the wrong."
author: Earl of Roscommon

quote: "Let us consider the reason of the case. For nothing is law that is not reason."
author: Thomas Ken

quote: "A merry monarch, scandalous and poor."
author: Earl of Rochester

quote: "Of all those arts in which the wise excel, Nature's chief masterpiece is writing well."
author: Sheffield, Duke of Buckinghamshire

quote: "A man who could make so vile a pun would not scruple to pick a pocket."
author: John Dennis
source: The Gentleman's Magazine. Vol. li. Page 324

quote: "It is a maxim with me that no man was ever written out of reputation but by himself."
author: Richard Bentley

quote: "His cogitative faculties immersed In cogibundity of cogitation."
author: Henry Carey

quote: "Genteel in personage, Conduct, and equipage; Noble by heritage, Generous and free."
author: Henry Carey

quote: "What a monstrous tail our cat has got!"
author: Henry Carey
source: The Dragon of Wantley. Act ii. Sc. 1

quote: "Of all the girls that are so smart, There 's none like pretty Sally."
author: Henry Carey

quote: "Of all the days that 's in the week I dearly love but one day, And that 's the day that comes betwixt A Saturday and Monday."
author: Henry Carey

quote: "Our hopes, like towering falcons, aim At objects in an airy height; The little pleasure of the game Is from afar to view the flight."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "From ignorance our comfort flows. The only wretched are the wise."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Odds life! must one swear to the truth of a song?"
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Be to her virtues very kind; Be to her faults a little blind."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "That if weak women went astray, Their stars were more in fault than they."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "The end must justify the means."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "They never taste who always drink; They always talk who never think."
author: Matthew Prior
source: Upon a passage in the Scaligerana

quote: "That air and harmony of shape express, Fine by degrees, and beautifully less."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Now fitted the halter, now traversed the cart, And often took leave, but was loth to depart."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Nobles and heralds, by your leave, Here lies what once was Matthew Prior; The son of Adam and of Eve: Can Bourbon or Nassau claim higher?"
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Till their own dreams at length deceive 'em, And oft repeating, they believe 'em."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "Abra was ready ere I called her name; And though I called another, Abra came."
author: Matthew Prior
source: Solomon on the Vanity of the World. Book ii. Line 364

quote: "A Rechabite poor Will must live, And drink of Adam's ale."
author: Matthew Prior

quote: "We bear it calmly, though a ponderous woe, And still adore the hand that gives the blow."
author: John Pomfret
source: Verses to his Friend under Affliction

quote: "Libertas et natale solum: Fine words! I wonder where you stole 'em."
author: Jonathan Swift
source: Verses occasioned by Whitshed's Motto on his Coach

quote: "A college joke to cure the dumps."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "It is a maxim, that those to whom everybody allows the second place have an undoubted title to the first."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "Books, the children of the brain."
author: Jonathan Swift
source: Tale of a Tub. Sect. i

quote: "As boys do sparrows, with flinging salt upon their tails."
author: Jonathan Swift
source: Tale of a Tub. Sect. vii

quote: "He made it a part of his religion never to say grace to his meat."
author: Jonathan Swift
source: Tale of a Tub. Sect. xi

quote: "The two noblest things, which are sweetness and light."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "The reason why so few marriages are happy is because young ladies spend their time in making nets, not in making cages."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "Censure is the tax a man pays to the public for being eminent."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "A nice man is a man of nasty ideas."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "Do you think I was born in a wood to be afraid of an owl?"
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "The sight of you is good for sore eyes."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I hate nobody: I am in charity with the world."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I won't quarrel with my bread and butter."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She 's no chicken; she 's on the wrong side of thirty, if she be a day."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She looks as if butter wou'dn't melt in her mouth."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "If it had been a bear it would have bit you."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She wears her clothes as if they were thrown on with a pitchfork."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I mean you lie--under a mistake."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "He was a bold man that first eat an oyster."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "That is as well said as if I had said it myself."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "You must take the will for the deed."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "Fingers were made before forks, and hands before knives."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She has more goodness in her little finger than he has in his whole body."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "They say a carpenter 's known by his chips."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "The best doctors in the world are Doctor Diet, Doctor Quiet, and Doctor Merryman."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I 'll give you leave to call me anything, if you don't call me "spade."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "May you live all the days of your life."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I have fed like a farmer: I shall grow as fat as a porpoise."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I know Sir John will go, though he was sure it would rain cats and dogs."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "I thought you and he were hand-in-glove."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "There is none so blind as they that won't see."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She watches him as a cat would watch a mouse."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "She pays him in his own coin."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "There was all the world and his wife."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "There 's two words to that bargain."
author: Jonathan Swift

quote: "By magic numbers and persuasive sound."
author: William Congreve
source: The Mourning Bride. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "I came up stairs into the world, for I was born in a cellar."
author: William Congreve
source: Love for Love. Act ii. Sc. 7

quote: "Hannibal was a very pretty fellow in those days."
author: William Congreve
source: The Old Bachelor. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "Now, by St. Paul, the work goes bravely on."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "Off with his head! so much for Buckingham!"
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "And the ripe harvest of the new-mown hay Gives it a sweet and wholesome odour."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "With clink of hammers closing rivets up."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "A weak invention of the enemy."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "As good be out of the world as out of the fashion."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "Old houses mended, Cost little less than new before they 're ended."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "Possession is eleven points in the law."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "This business will never hold water."
author: Colley Cibber
source: She Wou'd and She Wou'd Not. Act iv

quote: "Losers must have leave to speak."
author: Colley Cibber

quote: "Blesses his stars and thinks it luxury."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "Were you with these, my prince, you 'd soon forget The pale, unripened beauties of the north."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "The woman that deliberates is lost."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "Curse all his virtues! they 've undone his country."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "When vice prevails, and impious men bear sway, The post of honour is a private station."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "And those that paint them truest praise them most."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "Should the whole frame of Nature round him break, In ruin and confusion hurled, He, unconcerned, would hear the mighty crack, And stand secure amidst a falling world."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "Much may be said on both sides."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "Round-heads and wooden-shoes are standing jokes."
author: Joseph Addison

quote: "As if Misfortune made the throne her seat, And none could be unhappy but the great."
author: Nicholas Rowe

quote: "At length the morn and cold indifference came."
author: Nicholas Rowe
source: The Fair Penitent. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "Is she not more than painting can express, Or youthful poets fancy when they love?"
author: Nicholas Rowe
source: The Fair Penitent. Act iii. Sc. 1

quote: "Is this that haughty gallant, gay Lothario?"
author: Nicholas Rowe
source: The Fair Penitent. Act v. Sc. i

quote: "A flower, when offered in the bud, Is no vain sacrifice."
author: Isaac Watts

quote: "And he that does one fault at first And lies to hide it, makes it two."
author: Isaac Watts

quote: "But, children, you should never let Such angry passions rise; Your little hands were never made To tear each other's eyes."
author: Isaac Watts

quote: "In books, or work, or healthful play."
author: Isaac Watts

quote: "From all who dwell below the skies Let the Creator's praise arise; Let the Redeemer's name be sung Through every land, by every tongue."
author: Isaac Watts

quote: "Fly, like a youthful hart or roe, Over the hills where spices grow."
author: Isaac Watts
source: Hymns and Spiritual Songs. Book i. Hymn 79

quote: "Strange that a harp of thousand strings Should keep in tune so long!"
author: Isaac Watts
source: Hymns and Spiritual Songs. Book ii. Hymn 19

quote: "The tall, the wise, the reverend head Must lie as low as ours."
author: Isaac Watts
source: Hymns and Spiritual Songs. Book ii. Hymn 63

quote: "When I can read my title clear To mansions in the skies, I 'll bid farewell to every fear, And wipe my weeping eyes."
author: Isaac Watts
source: Hymns and Spiritual Songs. Book ii. Hymn 65

quote: "There is a land of pure delight, Where saints immortal reign; Infinite day excludes the night, And pleasures banish pain."
author: Isaac Watts
source: Hymns and Spiritual Songs. Book ii. Hymn 66

quote: "Anything but history, for history must be false."
author: Sir Robert Walpole

quote: "I have read somewhere or other,--in Dionysius of Halicarnassus, I think,--that history is philosophy teaching by examples."
author: Viscount Bolingbroke
source: On the Study and Use of History. Letter 2

quote: "I believe they talked of me, for they laughed consumedly."
author: George Farquhar
source: The Beaux' Stratagem. Act iii. Sc. 1

quote: "'T was for the good of my country that I should be abroad."
author: George Farquhar
source: The Beaux' Stratagem. Act iii. Sc. 2

quote: "We call it only pretty Fanny's way."
author: Thomas Parnell
source: An Elegy to an Old Beauty

quote: "True as the needle to the pole, Or as the dial to the sun."
author: Barton Booth

quote: "The bell strikes one. We take no note of time But from its loss."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Poor pensioner on the bounties of an hour."
author: Edward Young

quote: "To waft a feather or to drown a fly."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Procrastination is the thief of time."
author: Edward Young

quote: "At thirty, man suspects himself a fool; Knows it at forty, and reforms his plan."
author: Edward Young

quote: "I 've lost a day!"--the prince who nobly cried, Had been an emperor without his crown."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Ah, how unjust to Nature and himself Is thoughtless, thankless, inconsistent man!"
author: Edward Young

quote: "The spirit walks of every day deceased."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Whose yesterdays look backwards with a smile."
author: Edward Young

quote: "How blessings brighten as they take their flight!"
author: Edward Young

quote: "Woes cluster. Rare are solitary woes; They love a train, they tread each other's heel."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Beautiful as sweet, And young as beautiful, and soft as young, And gay as soft, and innocent as gay!"
author: Edward Young

quote: "And feels a thousand deaths in fearing one."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Wishing, of all employments, is the worst."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Man wants but little, nor that little long."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Like our shadows, Our wishes lengthen as our sun declines."
author: Edward Young

quote: "That life is long which answers life's great end."
author: Edward Young

quote: "The man of wisdom is the man of years."
author: Edward Young

quote: "And all may do what has by man been done."
author: Edward Young

quote: "The man that blushes is not quite a brute."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Too low they build, who build beneath the stars."
author: Edward Young

quote: "A man of pleasure is a man of pains."
author: Edward Young

quote: "To frown at pleasure, and to smile in pain."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Some for renown, on scraps of learning dote, And think they grow immortal as they quote."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire i. Line 89

quote: "They that on glorious ancestors enlarge, Produce their debt instead of their discharge."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire i. Line 147

quote: "None think the great unhappy but the great."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire i. Line 238

quote: "Unlearned men of books assume the care, As eunuchs are the guardians of the fair."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire ii. Line 83

quote: "Be wise with speed; A fool at forty is a fool indeed."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire ii. Line 282

quote: "Think naught a trifle, though it small appear; Small sands the mountain, moments make the year, And trifles life."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire vi. Line 208

quote: "How commentators each dark passage shun, And hold their farthing candle to the sun."
author: Edward Young
source: Love of Fame. Satire vii. Line 97

quote: "The man that makes a character makes foes."
author: Edward Young
source: To Mr. Pope. Epistle i. Line 28

quote: "There buds the promise of celestial worth."
author: Edward Young

quote: "In records that defy the tooth of time."
author: Edward Young

quote: "Great let me call him, for he conquered me."
author: Edward Young

quote: "The sweetest garland to the sweetest maid."
author: Thomas Tickell
source: To a Lady with a Present of Flowers

quote: "I hear a voice you cannot hear, Which says I must not stay; I see a hand you cannot see, Which beckons me away."
author: Thomas Tickell

quote: "Together let us beat this ample field, Try what the open, what the covert yield."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 9

quote: "Seas roll to waft me, suns to light me rise; My footstool earth, my canopy the skies."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 139

quote: "Why has not man a microscopic eye? For this plain reason,--man is not a fly."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 193

quote: "The spider's touch, how exquisitely fine! Feels at each thread, and lives along the line."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 217

quote: "Remembrance and reflection how allied! What thin partitions sense from thought divide!"
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 225

quote: "Warms in the sun, refreshes in the breeze, Glows in the stars, and blossoms in the trees."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle i. Line 271

quote: "On life's vast ocean diversely we sail, Reason the card, but passion is the gale."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle ii. Line 107

quote: "The young disease, that must subdue at length, Grows with his growth, and strengthens with his strength."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle ii. Line 135

quote: "Extremes in nature equal ends produce; In man they join to some mysterious use."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle ii. Line 205

quote: "Learn of the little nautilus to sail, Spread the thin oar, and catch the driving gale."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iii. Line 177

quote: "The enormous faith of many made for one."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iii. Line 242

quote: "Reason's whole pleasure, all the joys of sense, Lie in three words,--health, peace, and competence."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 79

quote: "Honour and shame from no condition rise; Act well your part, there all the honour lies."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 193

quote: "Worth makes the man, and want of it the fellow; The rest is all but leather or prunello."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 203

quote: "Know then this truth (enough for man to know),-- "Virtue alone is happiness below."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 309

quote: "Say, shall my little bark attendant sail, Pursue the triumph and partake the gale?"
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 385

quote: "That virtue only makes our bliss below, And all our knowledge is ourselves to know."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Man. Epistle iv. Line 397

quote: "To observations which ourselves we make, We grow more partial for th' observer's sake."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Like following life through creatures you dissect, You lose it in the moment you detect."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "In vain sedate reflections we would make When half our knowledge we must snatch, not take."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Not always actions show the man; we find Who does a kindness is not therefore kind."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Manners with fortunes, humours turn with climes, Tenets with books, and principles with times."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Odious! in woollen! 't would a saint provoke," Were the last words that poor Narcissa spoke."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Choose a firm cloud before it fall, and in it Catch, ere she change, the Cynthia of this minute."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Fine by defect, and delicately weak."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "With too much quickness ever to be taught; With too much thinking to have common thought."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Virtue she finds too painful an endeavour, Content to dwell in decencies forever."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Men, some to business, some to pleasure take; But every woman is at heart a rake."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "See how the world its veterans rewards! A youth of frolics, an old age of cards."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Most women have no characters at all."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Woman 's at best a contradiction still."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Who shall decide when doctors disagree, And soundest casuists doubt, like you and me?"
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "The ruling passion, be it what it will, The ruling passion conquers reason still."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Extremes in Nature equal good produce; Extremes in man concur to general use."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "In the worst inn's worst room, with mat half hung."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Where London's column, pointing at the skies, Like a tall bully, lifts the head and lies."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "One science only will one genius fit: So vast is art, so narrow human wit."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part i. Line 60

quote: "Those oft are stratagems which errors seem, Nor is it Homer nods, but we that dream."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part i. Line 177

quote: "A little learning is a dangerous thing; Drink deep, or taste not the Pierian spring: There shallow draughts intoxicate the brain, And drinking largely sobers us again."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part ii. Line 15

quote: "Words are like leaves; and where they most abound, Much fruit of sense beneath is rarely found."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part ii. Line 109

quote: "A needless Alexandrine ends the song, That like a wounded snake drags its slow length along."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part ii. Line 156

quote: "Envy will merit as its shade pursue, But like a shadow proves the substance true."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part ii. Line 266

quote: "The bookful blockhead, ignorantly read, With loads of learned lumber in his head."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part iii. Line 53

quote: "Most authors steal their works, or buy; Garth did not write his own Dispensary."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part iii. Line 59

quote: "Led by the light of the Mæonian star."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Essay on Criticism. Part iii. Line 89

quote: "On her white breast a sparkling cross she wore Which Jews might kiss, and infidels adore."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto ii. Line 7

quote: "If to her share some female errors fall, Look on her face, and you 'll forget them all."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto ii. Line 17

quote: "At every word a reputation dies."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto iii. Line 16

quote: "The hungry judges soon the sentence sign, And wretches hang that jurymen may dine."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto iii. Line 21

quote: "Coffee, which makes the politician wise, And see through all things with his half-shut eyes."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto iii. Line 117

quote: "Sir Plume, of amber snuff-box justly vain, And the nice conduct of a clouded cane."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Rape of the Lock. Canto iv. Line 123

quote: "Fire in each eye, and papers in each hand, They rave, recite, and madden round the land."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 5

quote: "E'en Sunday shines no Sabbath day to me."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 12

quote: "Friend to my life, which did not you prolong, The world had wanted many an idle song."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 27

quote: "Obliged by hunger and request of friends."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 44

quote: "Fired that the house rejects him, "'Sdeath! I 'll print it, And shame the fools."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 61

quote: "No creature smarts so little as a fool."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 84

quote: "Destroy his fib or sophistry--in vain! The creature 's at his dirty work again."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 91

quote: "Means not, but blunders round about a meaning; And he whose fustian 's so sublimely bad, It is not poetry, but prose run mad."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 186

quote: "Should such a man, too fond to rule alone, Bear, like the Turk, no brother near the throne."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 197

quote: "Who but must laugh, if such a man there be? Who would not weep, if Atticus were he?"
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 213

quote: "On wings of winds came flying all abroad."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 218

quote: "Satire or sense, alas! can Sporus feel? Who breaks a butterfly upon a wheel?"
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 307

quote: "Wit that can creep, and pride that licks the dust."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Epistle to Dr. Arbuthnot. Prologue to the Satires. Line 333

quote: "Bare the mean heart that lurks behind a star."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Satire i. Book ii. Line 110

quote: "For I, who hold sage Homer's rule the best, Welcome the coming, speed the going guest."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Satire ii. Book ii. Line 159

quote: "Give me again my hollow tree, A crust of bread, and liberty."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Satire vi. Book ii. Line 220

quote: "When the brisk minor pants for twenty-one."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book i. Line 38

quote: "He 's armed without that 's innocent within."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book i. Line 94

quote: "Get place and wealth, if possible, with grace; If not, by any means get wealth and place."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book i. Line 103

quote: "Above all Greek, above all Roman fame."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 26

quote: "Authors, like coins, grow dear as they grow old."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 35

quote: "The mob of gentlemen who wrote with ease."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 108

quote: "One simile that solitary shines In the dry desert of a thousand lines."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 111

quote: "Who says in verse what others say in prose."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 202

quote: "E'en copious Dryden wanted or forgot The last and greatest art,--the art to blot."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 280

quote: "Who pants for glory finds but short repose: A breath revives him, or a breath o'erthrows."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 300

quote: "There still remains to mortify a wit The many-headed monster of the pit."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle i. Book ii. Line 304

quote: "Years following years steal something every day; At last they steal us from ourselves away."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle ii. Book ii. Line 72

quote: "Words that wise Bacon or brave Raleigh spoke."
author: Alexander Pope
source: Satires, Epistles, and Odes of Horace. Epistle ii. Book ii. Line 168

quote: "Poetic Justice, with her lifted scale, Where in nice balance truth with gold she weighs, And solid pudding against empty praise."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "While pensive poets painful vigils keep, Sleepless themselves to give their readers sleep."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "How index-learning turns no student pale, Yet holds the eel of science by the tail."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "A wit with dunces, and a dunce with wits."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "How sweet an Ovid, Murray was our boast!"
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "E'en Palinurus nodded at the helm."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Curse on all laws but those which love has made! Love, free as air at sight of human ties, Spreads his light wings, and in a moment flies."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "And love the offender, yet detest the offence."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "How happy is the blameless vestal's lot! The world forgetting, by the world forgot."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "He best can paint them who shall feel them most."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "A mighty hunter, and his prey was man."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "From old Belerium to the northern main."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Nor Fame I slight, nor for her favours call; She comes unlooked for if she comes at all."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Teach me to feel another's woe, To hide the fault I see; That mercy I to others show, That mercy show to me."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Happy the man whose wish and care A few paternal acres bound."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Of manners gentle, of affections mild; In wit a man, simplicity a child."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Love seldom haunts the breast where learning lies, And Venus sets ere Mercury can rise."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Wife of Bath. Her Prologue. Line 369

quote: "You beat your pate, and fancy wit will come; Knock as you please, there 's nobody at home."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "For he lives twice who can at once employ The present well, and e'en the past enjoy."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Who dared to love their country, and be poor."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Party is the madness of many for the gain of a few."
author: Alexander Pope

quote: "Thick as autumnal leaves or driving sand."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book ii. Line 970

quote: "Ajax the great . . . Himself a host."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book iii. Line 293

quote: "First in the fight and every graceful deed."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book iv. Line 295

quote: "The first in banquets, but the last in fight."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book iv. Line 401

quote: "With all its beauteous honours on its head."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book iv. Line 557

quote: "A wealthy priest, but rich without a fault."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book v. Line 16

quote: "Not two strong men the enormous weight could raise,-- Such men as live in these degenerate days."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book v. Line 371

quote: "He held his seat,--a friend to human race."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book vi. Line 18

quote: "If yet not lost to all the sense of shame."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book vi. Line 350

quote: "The young Astyanax, the hope of Troy."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book vi. Line 467

quote: "Aurora now, fair daughter of the dawn, Sprinkled with rosy light the dewy lawn."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book viii. Line 1

quote: "Short is my date, but deathless my renown."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book ix. Line 535

quote: "A generous friendship no cold medium knows, Burns with one love, with one resentment glows."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book ix. Line 725

quote: "To labour is the lot of man below; And when Jove gave us life, he gave us woe."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book x. Line 78

quote: "Content to follow when we lead the way."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book x. Line 141

quote: "He serves me most who serves his country best."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book x. Line 201

quote: "Praise from a friend, or censure from a foe, Are lost on hearers that our merits know."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book x. Line 293

quote: "The life which others pay let us bestow, And give to fame what we to nature owe."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xii. Line 393

quote: "The best of things beyond their measure cloy."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xiii. Line 795

quote: "To hide their ignominious heads in Troy."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xiv. Line 170

quote: "Persuasive speech, and more persuasive sighs, Silence that spoke, and eloquence of eyes."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xiv. Line 251

quote: "The mildest manners, and the gentlest heart."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xvii. Line 756

quote: "I live an idle burden to the ground."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xviii. Line 134

quote: "Our business in the field of fight Is not to question, but to prove our might."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xx. Line 304

quote: "A mass enormous! which in modern days No two of earth's degenerate sons could raise."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xx. Line 337

quote: "The bitter dregs of fortune's cup to drain."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxii. Line 85

quote: "Who dies in youth and vigour, dies the best."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxii. Line 100

quote: "No season now for calm familiar talk."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxii. Line 169

quote: "A green old age, unconscious of decays, That proves the hero born in better days."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxiii. Line 929

quote: "Two urns by Jove's high throne have ever stood,-- The source of evil one, and one of good."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxiv. Line 663

quote: "The mildest manners with the bravest mind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Iliad of Homer. Book xxiv. Line 963

quote: "Few sons attain the praise Of their great sires, and most their sires disgrace."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book ii. Line 315

quote: "Urge him with truth to frame his fair replies; And sure he will: for Wisdom never lies."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iii. Line 25

quote: "A faultless body and a blameless mind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iii. Line 138

quote: "The long historian of my country's woes."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iii. Line 142

quote: "Forgetful youth! but know, the Power above With ease can save each object of his love; Wide as his will extends his boundless grace."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iii. Line 285

quote: "There with commutual zeal we both had strove In acts of dear benevolence and love: Brothers in peace, not rivals in command."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 241

quote: "The glory of a firm, capacious mind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 262

quote: "Wise to resolve, and patient to perform."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 372

quote: "Forget the brother, and resume the man."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 732

quote: "Gentle of speech, beneficent of mind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 917

quote: "The people's parent, he protected all."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 921

quote: "The big round tear stands trembling in her eye."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 936

quote: "The windy satisfaction of the tongue."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book iv. Line 1092

quote: "Jove weighs affairs of earth in dubious scales, And the good suffers while the bad prevails."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book vi. Line 229

quote: "By Jove the stranger and the poor are sent, And what to those we give, to Jove is lent."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book vi. Line 247

quote: "A decent boldness ever meets with friends."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book vii. Line 67

quote: "Whose well-taught mind the present age surpast."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book vii. Line 210

quote: "In youth and beauty wisdom is but rare!"
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book vii. Line 379

quote: "A gen'rous heart repairs a sland'rous tongue."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book viii. Line 432

quote: "Strong are her sons, though rocky are her shores."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book ix. Line 28

quote: "Respect us human, and relieve us poor."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book ix. Line 318

quote: "Rare gift! but oh what gift to fools avails!"
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book x. Line 29

quote: "And not a man appears to tell their fate."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book x. Line 308

quote: "Born but to banquet, and to drain the bowl."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book x. Line 662

quote: "Thin airy shoals of visionary ghosts."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xi. Line 48

quote: "The first in glory, as the first in place."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xi. Line 441

quote: "Up the high hill he heaves a huge round stone."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xi. Line 736

quote: "There in the bright assemblies of the skies."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xi. Line 745

quote: "And what so tedious as a twice-told tale."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xii. Line 538

quote: "How prone to doubt, how cautious are the wise!"
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xiii. Line 375

quote: "It never was our guise To slight the poor, or aught humane despise."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xiv. Line 65

quote: "The sex is ever to a soldier kind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xiv. Line 246

quote: "Far from gay cities and the ways of men."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xiv. Line 410

quote: "Who love too much, hate in the like extreme, And both the golden mean alike condemn."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xv. Line 79

quote: "True friendship's laws are by this rule exprest,-- Welcome the coming, speed the parting guest."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xv. Line 83

quote: "Discourse, the sweeter banquet of the mind."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xv. Line 433

quote: "Whatever day Makes man a slave, takes half his worth away."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xvii. Line 392

quote: "A winy vapour melting in a tear."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xix. Line 143

quote: "The ruins of himself! now worn away With age, yet still majestic in decay."
author: Alexander Pope
source: The Odyssey of Homer. Book xxiv. Line 271

quote: "Over the hills and far away."
author: John Gay
source: The Beggar's Opera. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "The fly that sips treacle is lost in the sweets."
author: John Gay
source: The Beggar's Opera. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "Brother, brother! we are both in the wrong."
author: John Gay
source: The Beggar's Opera. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "How happy could I be with either, Were t' other dear charmer away!"
author: John Gay
source: The Beggar's Opera. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "Adieu, she cried, and waved her lily hand."
author: John Gay
source: Sweet William's Farewell to Black-eyed Susan

quote: "Where yet was ever found a mother Who 'd give her booby for another?"
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Mother, the Nurse, and the Fairy

quote: "Lest men suspect your tale untrue, Keep probability in view."
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Painter who pleased Nobody and Everybody

quote: "In ev'ry age and clime we see Two of a trade can never agree."
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Rat-catcher and Cats

quote: "Is there no hope? the sick man said; The silent doctor shook his head."
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Sick Man and the Angel

quote: "While there is life there 's hope, he cried."
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Sick Man and the Angel

quote: "And when a lady 's in the case, You know all other things give place."
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part i. The Hare and many Friends

quote: "From wine what sudden friendship springs!"
author: John Gay
source: Fables. Part ii. The Squire and his Cur

quote: "Life is a jest, and all things show it; I thought so once, but now I know it."
author: John Gay

quote: "Let this great maxim be my virtue's guide,-- In part she is to blame that has been tried: He comes too near that comes to be denied."
author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu

quote: "And we meet, with champagne and a chicken, at last."
author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu

quote: "Be plain in dress, and sober in your diet; In short, my deary, kiss me, and be quiet."
author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu
source: A Summary of Lord Lyttelton's Advice

quote: "Satire should, like a polished razor keen, Wound with a touch that 's scarcely felt or seen."
author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu
source: To the Imitator of the First Satire of Horace. Book ii

quote: "But the fruit that can fall without shaking Indeed is too mellow for me."
author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu

quote: "Take time enough: all other graces Will soon fill up their proper places."
author: John Byrom

quote: "The point is plain as a pike-staff."
author: John Byrom

quote: "None but himself can be his parallel."
author: Louis Theobald

quote: "Whatever is worth doing at all, is worth doing well."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "I knew once a very covetous, sordid fellow, who used to say, "Take care of the pence, for the pounds will take care of themselves."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "Style is the dress of thoughts."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "I assisted at the birth of that most significant word "flirtation," which dropped from the most beautiful mouth in the world."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "Unlike my subject now shall be my song; It shall be witty, and it sha'n't be long."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "The dews of the evening most carefully shun,-- Those tears of the sky for the loss of the sun."
author: Earl of Chesterfield
source: Advice to a Lady in Autumn

quote: "The nation looked upon him as a deserter, and he shrunk into insignificancy and an earldom."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "He adorned whatever subject he either spoke or wrote upon, by the most splendid eloquence."
author: Earl of Chesterfield

quote: "Fling but a stone, the giant dies."
author: Matthew Green

quote: "Thus I steer my bark, and sail On even keel, with gentle gale."
author: Matthew Green

quote: "Though pleased to see the dolphins play, I mind my compass and my way."
author: Matthew Green

quote: "He lives to build, not boast, a generous race; No tenth transmitter of a foolish face."
author: Richard Savage

quote: "The schoolboy, with his satchel in his hand, Whistling aloud to bear his courage up."
author: Robert Blair

quote: "Of joys departed, Not to return, how painful the remembrance!"
author: Robert Blair

quote: "Base Envy withers at another's joy, And hates that excellence it cannot reach."
author: James Thomson

quote: "Amid the roses fierce Repentance rears Her snaky crest."
author: James Thomson

quote: "Delightful task! to rear the tender thought, To teach the young idea how to shoot."
author: James Thomson

quote: "The meek-ey'd Morn appears, mother of dews."
author: James Thomson

quote: "Falsely luxurious, will not man awake?"
author: James Thomson

quote: "For many a day, and many a dreadful night, Incessant lab'ring round the stormy cape."
author: James Thomson

quote: "A lucky chance, that oft decides the fate Of mighty monarchs."
author: James Thomson

quote: "So stands the statue that enchants the world, So bending tries to veil the matchless boast, The mingled beauties of exulting Greece."
author: James Thomson

quote: "See, Winter comes to rule the varied year."
author: James Thomson

quote: "From seeming evil still educing good."
author: James Thomson

quote: "Come then, expressive silence, muse His praise."
author: James Thomson

quote: "O fair undress, best dress! it checks no vein, But every flowing limb in pleasure drowns, And heightens ease with grace."
author: James Thomson
source: The Castle of Indolence. Canto i. Stanza 26

quote: "A bard here dwelt, more fat than bard beseems."
author: James Thomson
source: The Castle of Indolence. Canto i. Stanza 68

quote: "Health is the vital principle of bliss, And exercise, of health."
author: James Thomson
source: The Castle of Indolence. Canto ii. Stanza 55

quote: "Ever charming, ever new, When will the landscape tire the view?"
author: John Dyer

quote: "That execrable sum of all villanies commonly called a Slave Trade."
author: John Wesley

quote: "Yet still we hug the dear deceit."
author: Nathaniel Cotton

quote: "All Nature wears one universal grin."
author: Henry Fielding
source: Tom Thumb the Great. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "To sun myself in Huncamunca's eyes."
author: Henry Fielding
source: Tom Thumb the Great. Act i. Sc. 3

quote: "Much may be said on both sides."
author: Henry Fielding
source: The Covent Garden Tragedy. Act i. Sc. 8

quote: "We must eat to live and live to eat."
author: Henry Fielding

quote: "Oh, the roast beef of England, And old England's roast beef!"
author: Henry Fielding
source: The Grub Street Opera. Act iii. Sc. 2

quote: "This story will not go down."
author: Henry Fielding

quote: "Confidence is a plant of slow growth in an aged bosom."
author: William Pitt, Earl of Chatham

quote: "A long train of these practices has at length unwillingly convinced me that there is something behind the throne greater than the King himself."
author: William Pitt, Earl of Chatham

quote: "Reparation for our rights at home, and security against the like future violations."
author: William Pitt, Earl of Chatham
source: Letter to the Earl of Shelburne, Sept. 29, 1770

quote: "If I were an American, as I am an Englishman, while a foreign troop was landed in my country I never would lay down my arms,--never! never! never!"
author: William Pitt, Earl of Chatham

quote: "Let observation with extensive view Survey mankind, from China to Peru."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "There mark what ills the scholar's life assail,-- Toil, envy, want, the patron, and the jail."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "He left the name at which the world grew pale, To point a moral, or adorn a tale."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Hides from himself his state, and shuns to know That life protracted is protracted woe."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Superfluous lags the veteran on the stage."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Fears of the brave, and follies of the wise! From Marlb'rough's eyes the streams of dotage flow, And Swift expires, a driv'ler and a show."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Must helpless man, in ignorance sedate, Roll darkling down the torrent of his fate?"
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Studious to please, yet not ashamed to fail."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Prologue to the Tragedy of Irene

quote: "For we that live to please must please to live."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Prologue on the Opening of Drury Lane Theatre

quote: "Catch, then, oh catch the transient hour; Improve each moment as it flies! Life 's a short summer, man a flower; He dies--alas! how soon he dies!"
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "That saw the manners in the face."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Lines on the Death of Hogarth

quote: "A Poet, Naturalist, and Historian, Who left scarcely any style of writing untouched, And touched nothing that he did not adorn."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Trade's proud empire hastes to swift decay."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Line added to Goldsmith's Deserted Village

quote: "A man used to vicissitudes is not easily dejected."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Few things are impossible to diligence and skill."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Knowledge is more than equivalent to force."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "I live in the crowd of jollity, not so much to enjoy company as to shun myself."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Many things difficult to design prove easy to performance."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "The first years of man must make provision for the last."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Example is always more efficacious than precept."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "The endearing elegance of female friendship."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Whoever wishes to attain an English style, familiar but not coarse, and elegant but not ostentatious, must give his days and nights to the volumes of Addison."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "The trappings of a monarchy would set up an ordinary commonwealth."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "He is no wise man that will quit a certainty for an uncertainty."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "What is read twice is commonly better remembered than what is transcribed."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Being in a ship is being in a jail, with the chance of being drowned."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. iii. 1759

quote: "The noblest prospect which a Scotchman ever sees is the high-road that leads him to England."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. v. 1763

quote: "If he does really think that there is no distinction between virtue and vice, why, sir, when he leaves our houses let us count our spoons."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. v. 1763

quote: "Sir, your levellers wish to level _down_ as far as themselves; but they cannot bear levelling _up_ to themselves."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. v. 1763

quote: "A man ought to read just as inclination leads him; for what he reads as a task will do him little good."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. vi. 1763

quote: "Sir, a woman preaching is like a dog's walking on his hind legs. It is not done well; but you are surprised to find it done at all."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. ix

quote: "I look upon it, that he who does not mind his belly will hardly mind anything else."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. ix

quote: "This was a good dinner enough, to be sure, but it was not a dinner to _ask_ a man to."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. ii. Chap. ix

quote: "It matters not how a man dies, but how he lives."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iii. Chap. iv

quote: "That fellow seems to me to possess but one idea, and that is a wrong one."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iii. Chap. v. 1770

quote: "I am a great friend to public amusements; for they keep people from vice."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iii. Chap. viii. 1772

quote: "A cow is a very good animal in the field; but we turn her out of a garden."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iii. Chap. viii. 1772

quote: "Much may be made of a Scotchman if he be caught young."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iii. Chap. viii. 1772

quote: "A man may write at any time if he will set himself doggedly to it."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. iv. Chap. ii. 1773

quote: "Was ever poet so trusted before?"
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. v. Chap. vi. 1774

quote: "Attack is the reaction. I never think I have hit hard unless it rebounds."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. v. Chap. vi. 1775

quote: "A man will turn over half a library to make one book."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. v. Chap. viii. 1775

quote: "Patriotism is the last refuge of a scoundrel."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. v. Chap. ix

quote: "Knowledge is of two kinds: we know a subject ourselves, or we know where we can find information upon it."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. v. Chap. ix

quote: "I never take a nap after dinner but when I have had a bad night; and then the nap takes me."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. i. 1775

quote: "In lapidary inscriptions a man is not upon oath."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. i. 1775

quote: "There is nothing which has yet been contrived by man by which so much happiness is produced as by a good tavern or inn."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. iii. 1776

quote: "No man but a blockhead ever wrote except for money."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. iii. 1776

quote: "Questioning is not the mode of conversation among gentlemen."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. iv. 1776

quote: "A man is very apt to complain of the ingratitude of those who have risen far above him."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. iv. 1776

quote: "All this [wealth] excludes but one evil,--poverty."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. ix. 1777

quote: "Employment, sir, and hardships prevent melancholy."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. ix. 1777

quote: "When a man is tired of London he is tired of life; for there is in London all that life can afford."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. ix. 1777

quote: "He was so generally civil that nobody thanked him for it."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vi. Chap. ix. 1777

quote: "Goldsmith, however, was a man who whatever he wrote, did it better than any other man could do."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vii. Chap. iii. 1778

quote: "The true, strong, and sound mind is the mind that can embrace equally great things and small."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vii. Chap. vi. 1778

quote: "Claret is the liquor for boys, port for men; but he who aspires to be a hero must drink brandy."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vii. Chap. viii. 1779

quote: "A Frenchman must be always talking, whether he knows anything of the matter or not; an Englishman is content to say nothing when he has nothing to say."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vii. Chap. x

quote: "Of Dr. Goldsmith he said, "No man was more foolish when he had not a pen in his hand, or more wise when he had."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. vii. Chap. x

quote: "The potentiality of growing rich beyond the dreams of avarice."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. ii

quote: "Classical quotation is the _parole_ of literary men all over the world."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. iii. 1781

quote: "My friend was of opinion that when a man of rank appeared in that character [as an author], he deserved to have his merits handsomely allowed."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. iii. 1781

quote: "I never have sought the world; the world was not to seek me."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. v. 1783

quote: "He is not only dull himself, but the cause of dullness in others."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. v. 1784

quote: "You see they 'd have fitted him to a T."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. ix. 1784

quote: "I have found you an argument; I am not obliged to find you an understanding."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. ix. 1784

quote: "Who drives fat oxen should himself be fat."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. ix. 1784

quote: "Blown about with every wind of criticism."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Life of Johnson_ (Boswell). _Vol. viii. Chap. x. 1784

quote: "The law is the last result of human wisdom acting upon human experience for the benefit of the public."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "The use of travelling is to regulate imagination by reality, and instead of thinking how things may be, to see them as they are."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Dictionaries are like watches; the worst is better than none, and the best cannot be expected to go quite true."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Books that you may carry to the fire and hold readily in your hand, are the most useful after all."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Abstinence is as easy to me as temperance would be difficult."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Hawkesworth said of Johnson, "You have a memory that would convict any author of plagiarism in any court of literature in the world."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "His conversation does not show the minute-hand, but he strikes the hour very correctly."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "I am very fond of the company of ladies. I like their beauty, I like their delicacy, I like their vivacity, and I like their silence."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "This world, where much is to be done and little to be known."
author: Samuel Johnson
source: Prayers and Meditations. Against inquisitive and perplexing Thoughts

quote: "Gratitude is a fruit of great cultivation; you do not find it among gross people."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "A fellow that makes no figure in company, and has a mind as narrow as the neck of a vinegar-cruet."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "Towering in the confidence of twenty-one."
author: Samuel Johnson

quote: "What is your sex's earliest, latest care, Your heart's supreme ambition? To be fair."
author: Lord Lyttleton

quote: "The lover in the husband may be lost."
author: Lord Lyttleton

quote: "How much the wife is dearer than the bride."
author: Lord Lyttleton

quote: "The maid who modestly conceals Her beauties, while she hides, reveals; Give but a glimpse, and fancy draws Whate'er the Grecian Venus was."
author: Edward Moore
source: The Spider and the Bee. Fable x

quote: "But from the hoop's bewitching round, Her very shoe has power to wound."
author: Edward Moore
source: The Spider and the Bee. Fable x

quote: "Time still, as he flies, brings increase to her truth, And gives to her mind what he steals from her youth."
author: Edward Moore

quote: "I am rich beyond the dreams of avarice."
author: Edward Moore

quote: "Our armies swore terribly in Flanders," cried my Uncle Toby, "but nothing to this."
author: Laurence Sterne
source: Tristram Shandy_ (orig. ed.). _Vol. iii. Chap. xi

quote: "They order," said I, "this matter better in France."
author: Laurence Sterne

quote: "Trust that man in nothing who has not a conscience in everything."
author: Laurence Sterne

quote: "So sweetly she bade me adieu, I thought that she bade me return."
author: William Shenstone

quote: "I have found out a gift for my fair; I have found where the wood-pigeons breed."
author: William Shenstone

quote: "Her cap, far whiter than the driven snow, Emblems right meet of decency does yield."
author: William Shenstone

quote: "A little bench of heedless bishops here, And there a chancellor in embryo."
author: William Shenstone

quote: "From humble Port to imperial Tokay."
author: James Townley
source: High Life below Stairs. Act ii. Sc. 1

quote: "What female heart can gold despise? What cat 's averse to fish?"
author: Thomas Gray
source: On the death of a Favourite Cat

quote: "They hear a voice in every wind, And snatch a fearful joy."
author: Thomas Gray
source: On a Distant Prospect of Eton College. Stanza 4

quote: "Gay hope is theirs by fancy fed, Less pleasing when possest; The tear forgot as soon as shed, The sunshine of the breast."
author: Thomas Gray
source: On a Distant Prospect of Eton College. Stanza 5

quote: "From Helicon's harmonious springs A thousand rills their mazy progress take."
author: Thomas Gray
source: The Progress of Poesy. I. 1, Line 3

quote: "To high-born Hoel's harp, or soft Llewellyn's lay."
author: Thomas Gray

quote: "Dear as the light that visits these sad eyes; Dear as the ruddy drops that warm my heart."
author: Thomas Gray

quote: "The still small voice of gratitude."
author: Thomas Gray

quote: "Each in his narrow cell forever laid, The rude forefathers of the hamlet sleep."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 4

quote: "The breezy call of incense-breathing morn."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 5

quote: "Where through the long-drawn aisle and fretted vault, The pealing anthem swells the note of praise."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 10
