quote: "Forbade to wade through slaughter to a throne, And shut the gates of mercy on mankind."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 17

quote: "Implores the passing tribute of a sigh."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 20

quote: "Brushing with hasty steps the dews away, To meet the sun upon the upland lawn."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Elegy in a Country Churchyard. Stanza 25

quote: "And weep the more, because I weep in vain."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Sonnet. On the Death of Mr. West

quote: "Rich windows that exclude the light, And passages that lead to nothing."
author: Thomas Gray

quote: "The hues of bliss more brightly glow, Chastised by sabler tints of woe."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Ode on the Pleasure arising from Vicissitude. Line 45

quote: "The meanest floweret of the vale, The simplest note that swells the gale, The common sun, the air, the skies, To him are opening paradise."
author: Thomas Gray
source: Ode on the Pleasure arising from Vicissitude. Line 53

quote: "The social smile, the sympathetic tear."
author: Thomas Gray

quote: "Corrupted freemen are the worst of slaves."
author: David Garrick

quote: "Their cause I plead,--plead it in heart and mind; A fellow-feeling makes one wondrous kind."
author: David Garrick
source: Prologue on Quitting the Stage in 1776

quote: "Hearts of oak are our ships, Hearts of oak are our men."
author: David Garrick

quote: "The world is a comedy to those that think, a tragedy to those who feel."
author: Horace Walpole

quote: "A careless song, with a little nonsense in it now and then, does not misbecome a monarch."
author: Horace Walpole

quote: "The whole [Scotch] nation hitherto has been void of wit and humour, and even incapable of relishing it."
author: Horace Walpole

quote: "In numbers warmly pure and sweetly strong."
author: William Collins

quote: "'T was sad by fits, by starts 't was wild."
author: William Collins

quote: "In notes by distance made more sweet."
author: William Collins

quote: "O Music! sphere-descended maid, Friend of Pleasure, Wisdom's aid!"
author: William Collins

quote: "Too nicely Jonson knew the critic's part; Nature in him was almost lost in Art."
author: William Collins
source: To Sir Thomas Hammer on his Edition of Shakespeare

quote: "Oft has it been my lot to mark A proud, conceited, talking spark."
author: James Merrick

quote: "He made him a hut, wherein he did put The carcass of Robinson Crusoe. O poor Robinson Crusoe!"
author: Samuel Foote
source: The Mayor of Garratt. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "Born in a cellar, and living in a garret."
author: Samuel Foote

quote: "Such and so various are the tastes of men."
author: Mark Akenside
source: Pleasures of the Imagination. Book iii. Line 567

quote: "Than Timoleon's arms require, And Tully's curule chair, and Milton's golden lyre."
author: Mark Akenside
source: Ode. On a Sermon against Glory. Stanza ii

quote: "Seeks painted trifles and fantastic toys, And eagerly pursues imaginary joys."
author: Mark Akenside

quote: "I 'll woo her as the lion wooes his brides."
author: John Home

quote: "A rude and boisterous captain of the sea."
author: John Home

quote: "The fattest hog in Epicurus' sty."
author: William Mason

quote: "Thus far we run before the wind."
author: Arthur Murphy

quote: "The flowers of the forest are a' wide awae."
author: Jane Elliott

quote: "Remote, unfriended, melancholy, slow, Or by the lazy Scheld or wandering Po."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "And learn the luxury of doing good."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Some fleeting good, that mocks me with the view."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "These little things are great to little man."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Creation's heir, the world, the world is mine!"
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Such is the patriot's boast, where'er we roam,-- His first, best country ever is at home."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Man seems the only growth that dwindles here."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Cheerful at morn, he wakes from short repose, Breasts the keen air, and carols as he goes."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "So the loud torrent and the whirlwind's roar But bind him to his native mountains more."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Pride in their port, defiance in their eye, I see the lords of humankind pass by."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "The land of scholars and the nurse of arms."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Laws grind the poor, and rich men rule the law."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Vain, very vain, my weary search to find That bliss which only centres in the mind."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Luke's iron crown, and Damien's bed of steel."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Sweet Auburn! loveliest village of the plain."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "The hawthorn bush, with seats beneath the shade, For talking age and whispering lovers made."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "The bashful virgin's sidelong looks of love."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "His best companions, innocence and health; And his best riches, ignorance of wealth."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "A man he was to all the country dear, And passing rich with forty pounds a year."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "The twelve good rules, the royal game of goose."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "To me more dear, congenial to my heart, One native charm, than all the gloss of art."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Her modest looks the cottage might adorn, Sweet as the primrose peeps beneath the thorn."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Through torrid tracts with fainting steps they go, Where wild Altama murmurs to their woe."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Such dainties to them, their health it might hurt; It 's like sending them ruffles when wanting a shirt."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Hope, like the gleaming taper's light, Adorns and cheers our way; And still, as darker grows the night, Emits a brighter ray."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Our Garrick 's a salad; for in him we see Oil, vinegar, sugar, and saltness agree!"
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "His conduct still right, with his argument wrong."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "A flattering painter, who made it his care To draw men as they ought to be, not as they are."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Here lies David Garrick, describe me who can, An abridgment of all that was pleasant in man."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "As a wit, if not first, in the very first line."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "On the stage he was natural, simple, affecting; 'T was only that when he was off he was acting."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "A kind and gentle heart he had, To comfort friends and foes; The naked every day he clad When he put on his clothes."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: Elegy on the Death of a Mad Dog

quote: "And in that town a dog was found, As many dogs there be, Both mongrel, puppy, whelp, and hound, And curs of low degree."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: Elegy on the Death of a Mad Dog

quote: "The dog, to gain his private ends, Went mad, and bit the man."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: Elegy on the Death of a Mad Dog

quote: "This same philosophy is a good horse in the stable, but an arrant jade on a journey."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "All his faults are such that one loves him still the better for them."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Measures, not men, have always been my mark."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "I love everything that 's old: old friends, old times, old manners, old books, old wine."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: She Stoops to Conquer. Act i

quote: "The genteel thing is the genteel thing any time, if as be that a gentleman bees in a concatenation accordingly."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: She Stoops to Conquer. Act i

quote: "I 'll be with you in the squeezing of a lemon."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: She Stoops to Conquer. Act i

quote: "Ask me no questions, and I 'll tell you no fibs."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: She Stoops to Conquer. Act iii

quote: "We sometimes had those little rubs which Providence sends to enhance the value of its favours."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "I find you want me to furnish you with argument and intellect too."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Taught by that Power that pities me, I learn to pity them."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Man wants but little here below, Nor wants that little long."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "And what is friendship but a name, A charm that lulls to sleep, A shade that follows wealth or fame, And leaves the wretch to weep?"
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "By the living jingo, she was all of a muck of sweat."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "They would talk of nothing but high life, and high-lived company, with other fashionable topics, such as pictures, taste, Shakespeare, and the musical glasses."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "To what happy accident is it that we owe so unexpected a visit?"
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "When lovely woman stoops to folly, And finds too late that men betray, What charm can soothe her melancholy? What art can wash her guilt away?"
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: The Hermit. On Woman. Chap. xxiv

quote: "To what fortuitous occurrence do we not owe every pleasure and convenience of our lives."
author: Oliver Goldsmith
source: The Hermit. On Woman. Chap. xxi

quote: "One writer, for instance, excels at a plan or a title-page, another works away the body of the book, and a third is a dab at an index."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "The true use of speech is not so much to express our wants as to conceal them."
author: Oliver Goldsmith

quote: "Every white will have its blacke, And every sweet its soure."
author: Thomas Percy
source: Reliques of Ancient Poetry. Sir Cauline

quote: "Late, late yestreen I saw the new moone, Wi' the auld moon in hir arme."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "He that had neyther been kith nor kin Might have seen a full fayre sight."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "Where gripinge grefes the hart wounde, And dolefulle dumps the mynde oppresse, There music with her silver sound With spede is wont to send redresse."
author: Thomas Percy
source: A Song to the Lute in Musicke

quote: "And how should I know your true love From many another one? Oh, by his cockle hat and staff, And by his sandal shoone."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "Sigh no more, ladies, sigh no more! Men were deceivers ever; One foot in sea and one on shore, To one thing constant never."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "He that would not when he might, He shall not when he wolda."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "When Arthur first in court began, And was approved king."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "Shall I bid her goe? What if I doe? Shall I bid her goe and spare not? Oh no, no, no! I dare not."
author: Thomas Percy

quote: "The writers against religion, whilst they oppose every system, are wisely careful never to set up any of their own."
author: Edmund Burke
source: A Vindication of Natural Society._[407-1] _Preface, vol. i. p. 7

quote: "I am convinced that we have a degree of delight, and that no small one, in the real misfortunes and pains of others."
author: Edmund Burke
source: On the Sublime and Beautiful. Sect. xiv. vol. 1. p. 118

quote: "In such a strait the wisest may well be perplexed and the boldest staggered."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Thoughts on the Cause of the Present Discontents. Vol. i. p. 516

quote: "When bad men combine, the good must associate; else they will fall one by one, an unpitied sacrifice in a contemptible struggle."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Thoughts on the Cause of the Present Discontents. Vol. i. p. 526

quote: "Of this stamp is the cant of, Not men, but measures."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Thoughts on the Cause of the Present Discontents. Vol. i. p. 531

quote: "The concessions of the weak are the concessions of fear."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 108

quote: "Fiction lags after truth, invention is unfruitful, and imagination cold and barren."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 116

quote: "A people who are still, as it were, but in the gristle, and not yet hardened into the bone of manhood."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 117

quote: "My vigour relents,--I pardon something to the spirit of liberty."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 118

quote: "I do not know the method of drawing up an indictment against a whole people."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 136

quote: "The march of the human mind is slow."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 149

quote: "All government,--indeed, every human benefit and enjoyment, every virtue and every prudent act,--is founded on compromise and barter."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Conciliation of America. Vol. ii. p. 169

quote: "They made and recorded a sort of institute and digest of anarchy, called the Rights of Man."
author: Edmund Burke
source: On the Army Estimates. Vol iii. p. 221

quote: "People will not look forward to posterity who never look backward to their ancestors."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 274

quote: "The unbought grace of life, the cheap defence of nations, the nurse of manly sentiment and heroic enterprise is gone."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 331

quote: "That chastity of honour which felt a stain like a wound."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 332

quote: "Vice itself lost half its evil by losing all its grossness."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 332

quote: "Kings will be tyrants from policy, when subjects are rebels from principle."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 334

quote: "Learning will be cast into the mire and trodden down under the hoofs of a swinish multitude."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 335

quote: "The men of England,--the men, I mean, of light and leading in England."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 365

quote: "He that wrestles with us strengthens our nerves and sharpens our skill. Our antagonist is our helper."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 453

quote: "To execute laws is a royal office; to execute orders is not to be a king. However, a political executive magistracy, though merely such, is a great trust."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Reflections on the Revolution in France. Vol. iii. p. 497

quote: "You can never plan the future by the past."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Letter to a Member of the National Assembly. Vol. iv. p. 55

quote: "The cold neutrality of an impartial judge."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Preface to Brissot's Address. Vol. v. p. 67

quote: "All men that are ruined, are ruined on the side of their natural propensities."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Letter i. On a Regicide Peace. Vol. v. p. 286

quote: "Example is the school of mankind, and they will learn at no other."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Letter i. On a Regicide Peace. Vol. v. p. 331

quote: "Early and provident fear is the mother of safety."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech on the Petition of the Unitarians. Vol. vii. p. 50

quote: "The people never give up their liberties but under some delusion."
author: Edmund Burke
source: Speech at County Meeting of Bucks, 1784

quote: "He mouths a sentence as curs mouth a bone."
author: Charles Churchill

quote: "With curious art the brain, too finely wrought, Preys on herself, and is destroyed by thought."
author: Charles Churchill

quote: "Men the most infamous are fond of fame, And those who fear not guilt yet start at shame."
author: Charles Churchill

quote: "Be England what she will, With all her faults she is my country still."
author: Charles Churchill

quote: "Is base in kind, and born to be a slave."
author: William Cowper

quote: "As if the world and they were hand and glove."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Happiness depends, as Nature shows, Less on exterior things than most suppose."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Freedom has a thousand charms to show, That slaves, howe'er contented, never know."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Elegant as simplicity, and warm As ecstasy."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Low ambition and the thirst of praise."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Made poetry a mere mechanic art."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Lights of the world, and stars of human race."
author: William Cowper

quote: "How much a dunce that has been sent to roam Excels a dunce that has been kept at home!"
author: William Cowper

quote: "Just knows, and knows no more, her Bible true,-- A truth the brilliant Frenchman never knew."
author: William Cowper

quote: "The sounding jargon of the schools."
author: William Cowper

quote: "A fool must now and then be right by chance."
author: William Cowper

quote: "He would not, with a peremptory tone, Assert the nose upon his face his own."
author: William Cowper

quote: "A moral, sensible, and well-bred man Will not affront me,--and no other can."
author: William Cowper

quote: "I cannot talk with civet in the room, A fine puss-gentleman that 's all perfume."
author: William Cowper

quote: "The solemn fop; significant and budge; A fool with judges, amongst fools a judge."
author: William Cowper

quote: "His wit invites you by his looks to come, But when you knock, it never is at home."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Our wasted oil unprofitably burns, Like hidden lamps in old sepulchral urns."
author: William Cowper

quote: "That good diffused may more abundant grow."
author: William Cowper

quote: "A business with an income at its heels Furnishes always oil for its own wheels."
author: William Cowper

quote: "An idler is a watch that wants both hands, As useless if it goes as if it stands."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Philologists, who chase A panting syllable through time and space, Start it at home, and hunt it in the dark To Gaul, to Greece, and into Noah's ark."
author: William Cowper

quote: "I am monarch of all I survey, My right there is none to dispute."
author: William Cowper
source: Verses supposed to be written by Alexander Selkirk

quote: "There goes the parson, O illustrious spark! And there, scarce less illustrious, goes the clerk."
author: William Cowper
source: On observing some Names of Little Note

quote: "I shall not ask Jean Jacques Rousseau If birds confabulate or no."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Misses! the tale that I relate This lesson seems to carry,-- Choose not alone a proper mate, But proper time to marry."
author: William Cowper

quote: "That though on pleasure she was bent, She had a frugal mind."
author: William Cowper

quote: "A hat not much the worse for wear."
author: William Cowper

quote: "The path of sorrow, and that path alone, Leads to the land where sorrow is unknown."
author: William Cowper

quote: "United yet divided, twain at once: So sit two kings of Brentford on one throne."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book i. The Sofa. Line 77

quote: "The earth was made so various, that the mind Of desultory man, studious of change And pleased with novelty, might be indulged."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book i. The Sofa. Line 506

quote: "Doing good, Disinterested good, is not our trade."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book i. The Sofa. Line 673

quote: "Mountains interposed Make enemies of nations who had else, Like kindred drops, been mingled into one."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 17

quote: "Slaves cannot breathe in England; if their lungs Receive our air, that moment they are free! They touch our country, and their shackles fall."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 40

quote: "Presume to lay their hand upon the ark Of her magnificent and awful cause."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 231

quote: "Praise enough To fill the ambition of a private man, That Chatham's language was his mother tongue."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 235

quote: "There is a pleasure in poetic pains Which only poets know."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 285

quote: "Transforms old print To zigzag manuscript, and cheats the eyes Of gallery critics by a thousand arts."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 363

quote: "Reading what they never wrote, Just fifteen minutes, huddle up their work, And with a well-bred whisper close the scene."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 411

quote: "Whoe'er was edified, themselves were not."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 444

quote: "Variety 's the very spice of life."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 606

quote: "She that asks Her dear five hundred friends."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book ii. The Timepiece. Line 642

quote: "Great contest follows, and much learned dust."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iii. The Garden. Line 161

quote: "From reveries so airy, from the toil Of dropping buckets into empty wells, And growing old in drawing nothing up."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iii. The Garden. Line 188

quote: "How various his employments whom the world Calls idle, and who justly in return Esteems that busy world an idler too!"
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iii. The Garden. Line 352

quote: "Who loves a garden loves a greenhouse too."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iii. The Garden. Line 566

quote: "What is it but a map of busy life, Its fluctuations, and its vast concerns?"
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening. Line 55

quote: "While fancy, like the finger of a clock, Runs the great circuit, and is still at home."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening. Line 118

quote: "O Winter, ruler of the inverted year!"
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening. Line 120

quote: "With spots quadrangular of diamond form, Ensanguined hearts, clubs typical of strife, And spades, the emblems of untimely graves."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening. Line 217

quote: "It seems the part of wisdom."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening. Line 336

quote: "Gloriously drunk, obey the important call."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book iv. The Winter Evening, Line 510

quote: "Silently as a dream the fabric rose, No sound of hammer or of saw was there."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book v. The Winter Morning Walk. Line 144

quote: "He is the freeman whom the truth makes free."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book v. The Winter Morning Walk. Line 733

quote: "Here the heart May give a useful lesson to the head, And Learning wiser grow without his books."
author: William Cowper
source: The Task. Book vi. Winter Walk at Noon. Line 85

quote: "Shine by the side of every path we tread With such a lustre, he that runs may read."
author: William Cowper

quote: "Behind a frowning providence He hides a shining face."
author: William Cowper

quote: "A worm is in the bud of youth, And at the root of age."
author: William Cowper
source: Stanzas subjoined to a Bill of Mortality

quote: "Toll for the brave!-- The brave that are no more! All sunk beneath the wave, Fast by their native shore!"
author: William Cowper
source: On the Loss of the Royal George

quote: "In sober state, Through the sequestered vale of rural life, The venerable patriarch guileless held The tenor of his way."
author: Beilby Porteus

quote: "Labour to keep alive in your breast that little spark of celestial fire,--conscience."
author: George Washington
source: Rule from the Copy-book of Washington when a schoolboy

quote: "The dews of summer nights did fall, The moon, sweet regent of the sky, Silvered the walls of Cumnor Hall And many an oak that grew thereby."
author: W. J. Mickle

quote: "There was a jolly miller once, Lived on the river Dee; He worked and sung from morn till night: No lark more blithe than he."
author: Isaac Bickerstaff
source: Love in a Village. Act i. Sc. 2

quote: "And this the burden of his song Forever used to be,-- I care for nobody, no, not I, If no one cares for me."
author: Isaac Bickerstaff
source: Love in a Village. Act i. Sc. 2

quote: "Young fellows will be young fellows."
author: Isaac Bickerstaff
source: Love in a Village. Act ii. Sc. 2

quote: "Ay, do despise me! I 'm the prouder for it; I like to be despised."
author: Isaac Bickerstaff

quote: "Ah, who can tell how hard it is to climb The steep where Fame's proud temple shines afar?"
author: James Beattie

quote: "Zealous, yet modest; innocent, though free; Patient of toil, serene amidst alarms; Inflexible in faith, invincible in arms."
author: James Beattie

quote: "Old age comes on apace to ravage all the clime."
author: James Beattie

quote: "He thought as a sage, though he felt as a man."
author: James Beattie

quote: "I am not a Virginian, but an American."
author: Patrick Henry
source: Speech in the Virginia Convention. September, 1774

quote: "Revenge is profitable, gratitude is expensive."
author: Edward Gibbon
source: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire_ (1776). _Chap. xi

quote: "In every deed of mischief he had a heart to resolve, a head to contrive, and a hand to execute."
author: Edward Gibbon
source: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire_ (1776). _Chap. xlviii

quote: "The winds and waves are always on the side of the ablest navigators."
author: Edward Gibbon
source: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire_ (1776). _Chap. lxviii

quote: "All that is human must retrograde if it do not advance."
author: Edward Gibbon
source: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire_ (1776). _Chap. lxxi

quote: "On the approach of spring I withdraw without reluctance from the noisy and extensive scene of crowds without company, and dissipation without pleasure."
author: Edward Gibbon

quote: "I was never less alone than when by myself."
author: Edward Gibbon

quote: "And the final event to himself [Mr. Burke] has been, that, as he rose like a rocket, he fell like the stick."
author: Thomas Paine

quote: "A fellow in a market town, Most musical, cried razors up and down."
author: John Wolcot

quote: "O give me the sweet shady side of Pall Mall!"
author: Charles Morris

quote: "A pampered menial drove me from the door."
author: Thomas Moss

quote: "It is to hope, though hope were lost."
author: Mrs. Barbauld

quote: "Error of opinion may be tolerated where reason is left free to combat it."
author: Thomas Jefferson

quote: "In the full tide of successful experiment."
author: Thomas Jefferson

quote: "When a man assumes a public trust, he should consider himself as public property."
author: Thomas Jefferson

quote: "Did you ever hear of Captain Wattle? He was all for love, and a little for the bottle."
author: Charles Dibdin

quote: "His form was of the manliest beauty, His heart was kind and soft; Faithful below he did his duty, But now he 's gone aloft."
author: Charles Dibdin

quote: "In men this blunder still you find,-- All think their little set mankind."
author: Hannah More

quote: "Small habits well pursued betimes May reach the dignity of crimes."
author: Hannah More

quote: "The elegant simplicity of the three per cents."
author: Lord Stowell
source: Lives of the Lord Chancellors_ (Campbell). _Vol. x. Chap. 212

quote: "Than all Bocara's vaunted gold, Than all the gems of Samarcand."
author: Sir William Jones

quote: "Go boldly forth, my simple lay, Whose accents flow with artless ease, Like orient pearls at random strung."
author: Sir William Jones

quote: "Illiterate him, I say, quite from your memory."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "A circulating library in a town is as an evergreen tree of diabolical knowledge."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "He is the very pine-apple of politeness!"
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "If I reprehend anything in this world, it is the use of my oracular tongue, and a nice derangement of epitaphs!"
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "As headstrong as an allegory on the banks of the Nile."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Our ancestors are very good kind of folks; but they are the last people I should choose to have a visiting acquaintance with."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "No caparisons, miss, if you please. Caparisons don't become a young woman."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "We will not anticipate the past; so mind, young people,--our retrospection will be all to the future."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "You are not like Cerberus, three gentlemen at once, are you?"
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "The quarrel is a very pretty quarrel as it stands; we should only spoil it by trying to explain it."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "You 're our enemy; lead the way, and we 'll precede."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "There 's nothing like being used to a thing."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "As there are three of us come on purpose for the game, you won't be so cantankerous as to spoil the party by sitting out."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "My valour is certainly going! it is sneaking off! I feel it oozing out, as it were, at the palm of my hands!"
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Steal! to be sure they may; and, egad, serve your best thoughts as gypsies do stolen children,--disfigure them to make 'em pass for their own."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Egad, I think the interpreter is the hardest to be understood of the two!"
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Sheer necessity,--the proper parent of an art so nearly allied to invention."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Certainly nothing is unnatural that is not physically impossible."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Where they _do_ agree on the stage, their unanimity is wonderful."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Inconsolable to the minuet in Ariadne."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "An oyster may be crossed in love."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "You shall see them on a beautiful quarto page, where a neat rivulet of text shall meander through a meadow of margin."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan
source: School for Scandal. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "Conscience has no more to do with gallantry than it has with politics."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "While his off-heel, insidiously aside. Provokes the caper which he seems to chide."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "Such protection as vultures give to lambs."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "A life spent worthily should be measured by a nobler line,--by deeds, not years."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan

quote: "The Right Honorable gentleman is indebted to his memory for his jests, and to his imagination for his facts."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan
source: Speech in Reply to Mr. Dundas. Sheridaniana

quote: "You write with ease to show your breeding, But easy writing 's curst hard reading."
author: Richard Brinsley Sheridan
source: Clio's Protest. Life of Sheridan_ (Moore). _Vol. i. p. 155

quote: "The hunter and the deer a shade."
author: Philip Freneau

quote: "In this fool's paradise he drank delight."
author: George Crabbe

quote: "Books cannot always please, however good; Minds are not ever craving for their food."
author: George Crabbe

quote: "In idle wishes fools supinely stay; Be there a will, and wisdom finds a way."
author: George Crabbe

quote: "Better to love amiss than nothing to have loved."
author: George Crabbe
source: Tales. Tale xiv. The Struggles of Conscience

quote: "'T was good advice, and meant, my son, Be good."
author: George Crabbe
source: Tales. Tale xxi. The Learned Boy

quote: "He tried the luxury of doing good."
author: George Crabbe
source: Tales of the Hall. Book iii. Boys at School

quote: "To sigh, yet not recede; to grieve, yet not repent."
author: George Crabbe
source: Tales of the Hall. Book iii. Boys at School

quote: "Time has touched me gently in his race, And left no odious furrows in my face."
author: George Crabbe
source: Tales of the Hall. Book xvii. The Widow

quote: "Perhaps it was right to dissemble your love, But--why did you kick me down stairs?"
author: J. P. Kemble

quote: "England expects every man to do his duty."
author: Horatio Nelson

quote: "Auld Nature swears the lovely dears Her noblest work she classes, O; Her 'prentice han' she tried on man, And then she made the lasses, O!"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Some books are lies frae end to end."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Some wee short hours ayont the twal."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "When chill November's surly blast Made fields and forests bare."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Gars auld claes look amaist as weel 's the new."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Beneath the milk-white thorn that scents the evening gale."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Perhaps Dundee's wild-warbling measures rise, Or plaintive Martyrs, worthy of the name."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "For a' that, and a' that, And twice as muckle 's a' that."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "And like a passing thought, she fled In light away."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Affliction's sons are brothers in distress; A brother to relieve,--how exquisite the bliss!"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "His locked, lettered, braw brass collar Showed him the gentleman and scholar."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Oh wad some power the giftie gie us To see oursel's as others see us! It wad frae monie a blunder free us, And foolish notion."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Then gently scan your brother man, Still gentler sister woman; Though they may gang a kennin' wrang, To step aside is human."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "What 's done we partly may compute, But know not what 's resisted."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Perhaps it may turn out a sang, Perhaps turn out a sermon."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "An atheist's laugh 's a poor exchange For Deity offended!"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "And may you better reck the rede, Than ever did the adviser!"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "If naebody care for me, I 'll care for naebody."
author: Robert Burns
source: I hae a Wife o' my Ain

quote: "Should auld acquaintance be forgot, And never brought to mind? Should auld acquaintance be forgot, And days o' lang syne?"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "We twa hae run about the braes, And pu'd the gowans fine."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "To make a happy fireside clime To weans and wife,-- That 's the true pathos and sublime Of human life."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "John Anderson my jo, John, When we were first acquent, Your locks were like the raven, Your bonny brow was brent."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "My heart 's in the Highlands, my heart is not here; My heart 's in the Highlands a-chasing the deer."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "She is a winsome wee thing, She is a handsome wee thing, She is a bonny wee thing, This sweet wee wife o' mine."
author: Robert Burns
source: My Wife 's a Winsome Wee Thing

quote: "In durance vile here must I wake and weep, And all my frowsy couch in sorrow steep."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Oh, my luve 's like a red, red rose, That 's newly sprung in June; Oh, my luve 's like the melodie That 's sweetly played in tune."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Contented wi' little, and cantie wi' mair."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Where sits our sulky, sullen dame, Gathering her brows like gathering storm, Nursing her wrath to keep it warm."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Ah, gentle dames! it gars me greet To think how monie counsels sweet, How monie lengthened sage advices, The husband frae the wife despises."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "The landlady and Tam grew gracious Wi' favours secret, sweet, and precious."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "The landlord's laugh was ready chorus."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "That hour, o' night's black arch the keystane."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "As Tammie glow'red, amazed and curious, The mirth and fun grew fast and furious."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "To see her is to love her, And love but her forever; For Nature made her what she is, And never made anither!"
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Chords that vibrate sweetest pleasure Thrill the deepest notes of woe."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "The rank is but the guinea's stamp, The man 's the gowd for a' that."
author: Robert Burns
source: For a' that and a' that

quote: "A prince can make a belted knight, A marquis, duke, and a' that; But an honest man 's aboon his might, Guid faith, he maunna fa' that."
author: Robert Burns
source: For a' that and a' that

quote: "Now a' is done that men can do, And a' is done in vain."
author: Robert Burns

quote: "Necessity is the argument of tyrants; it is the creed of slaves."
author: William Pitt
source: Speech on the India Bill, November, 1783

quote: "Loud roared the dreadful thunder, The rain a deluge showers."
author: Andrew Cherry

quote: "As she lay, on that day, In the bay of Biscay, O!"
author: Andrew Cherry

quote: "On their own merits modest men are dumb."
author: George Colman, The Younger
source: Epilogue to the Heir at Law

quote: "And what 's impossible can't be, And never, never comes to pass."
author: George Colman, The Younger

quote: "Three stories high, long, dull, and old, As great lords' stories often are."
author: George Colman, The Younger

quote: "But when ill indeed, E'en dismissing the doctor don't always succeed."
author: George Colman, The Younger

quote: "When taken, To be well shaken."
author: George Colman, The Younger

quote: "Thank you, good sir, I owe you one."
author: George Colman, The Younger
source: The Poor Gentleman. Act i. Sc. 2

quote: "Mynheer Vandunck, though he never was drunk, Sipped brandy and water gayly."
author: George Colman, The Younger

quote: "Rise with the lark, and with the lark to bed."
author: James Hurdis

quote: "The good are better made by ill, As odours crushed are sweeter still."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "Then never less alone than when alone."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "Mine be a cot beside the hill; A beehive's hum shall soothe my ear; A willowy brook that turns a mill, With many a fall, shall linger near."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "That very law which moulds a tear And bids it trickle from its source,-- That law preserves the earth a sphere, And guides the planets in their course."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "To vanish in the chinks that Time has made."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "Ward has no heart, they say, but I deny it: He has a heart, and gets his speeches by it."
author: Samuel Rogers

quote: "The princeps copy, clad in blue and gold."
author: John Ferriar

quote: "Now cheaply bought for thrice their weight in gold."
author: John Ferriar

quote: "Torn from their destined page (unworthy meed Of knightly counsel and heroic deed)."
author: John Ferriar

quote: "How pure the joy, when first my hands unfold The small, rare volume, black with tarnished gold!"
author: John Ferriar

quote: "His [Burke's] imperial fancy has laid all Nature under tribute, and has collected riches from every scene of the creation and every walk of art."
author: Robert Hall
source: Apology for the Freedom of the Press

quote: "Approbation from Sir Hubert Stanley is praise indeed."
author: Thomas Morton
source: A Cure for the Heartache. Act v. Sc. 2

quote: "The Commons, faithful to their system, remained in a wise and masterly inactivity."
author: Sir James Mackintosh

quote: "The frivolous work of polished idleness."
author: Sir James Mackintosh
source: Dissertation on Ethical Philosophy. Remarks on Thomas Brown

quote: "There 's nae sorrow there, John, There 's neither cauld nor care, John, The day is aye fair, In the land o' the leal."
author: Lady Nairne

quote: "Oh, we 're a' noddin', nid, nid, noddin'; Oh, we 're a' noddin' at our house at hame."
author: Lady Nairne

quote: "A penniless lass wi' a lang pedigree."
author: Lady Nairne

quote: "Our Federal Union: it must be preserved."
author: Andrew Jackson
source: Toast given on the Jefferson Birthday Celebration in 1830

quote: "You are uneasy; you never sailed with _me_ before, I see."
author: Andrew Jackson

quote: "Think of your forefathers! Think of your posterity!"
author: John Quincy Adams

quote: "This is the last of earth! I am content."
author: John Quincy Adams

quote: "It requires a surgical operation to get a joke well into a Scotch understanding."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 15

quote: "That knuckle-end of England,--that land of Calvin, oat-cakes, and sulphur."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 17

quote: "No one minds what Jeffrey says: . . . it is not more than a week ago that I heard him speak disrespectfully of the equator."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 17

quote: "We cultivate literature on a little oatmeal."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 23

quote: "It is always right that a man should be able to render a reason for the faith that is within him."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 53

quote: "Avoid shame, but do not seek glory,--nothing so expensive as glory."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 88

quote: "Looked as if she had walked straight out of the ark."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 157

quote: "The Smiths never had any arms, and have invariably sealed their letters with their thumbs."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 244

quote: "Not body enough to cover his mind decently with; his intellect is improperly exposed."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 258

quote: "He has spent all his life in letting down empty buckets into empty wells; and he is frittering away his age in trying to draw them up again."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 259

quote: "You find people ready enough to do the Samaritan, without the oil and twopence."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 261

quote: "Ah, you flavour everything; you are the vanilla of society."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 262

quote: "My living in Yorkshire was so far out of the way, that it was actually twelve miles from a lemon."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 262

quote: "As the French say, there are three sexes,--men, women, and clergymen."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 262

quote: "To take Macaulay out of literature and society and put him in the House of Commons, is like taking the chief physician out of London during a pestilence."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 265

quote: "Daniel Webster struck me much like a steam-engine in trousers."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 267

quote: "Macaulay is like a book in breeches. . . . He has occasional flashes of silence, that make his conversation perfectly delightful."
author: Sydney Smith
source: Lady Holland's Memoir. Vol. i. p. 363

quote: "In the four quarters of the globe, who reads an American book, or goes to an American play, or looks at an American picture or statue?"
author: Sydney Smith
source: Review of Seybert's Annals of the United States, 1820

quote: "Men who prefer any load of infamy, however great, to any pressure of taxation, however light."
author: Sydney Smith

quote: "Circumstances over which I have no control. I never saw so many shocking bad hats in my life."
author: Duke of Wellington
source: Upon seeing the first Reformed Parliament

quote: "There is no mistake; there has been no mistake; and there shall be no mistake."
author: Duke of Wellington

quote: "The man that lays his hand upon a woman, Save in the way of kindness, is a wretch Whom 't were gross flattery to name a coward."
author: John Tobin

quote: "She 's adorned Amply that in her husband's eye looks lovely,-- The truest mirror that an honest wife Can see her beauty in."
author: John Tobin

quote: "I called the New World into existence to redress the balance of the Old."
author: George Canning

quote: "No, here 's to the pilot that weathered the storm!"
author: George Canning
source: The Pilot that weathered the Storm

quote: "Too late I stayed,--forgive the crime! Unheeded flew the hours; How noiseless falls the foot of time That only treads on flowers."
author: William Robert Spencer

quote: "Action is transitory,--a step, a blow; The motion of a muscle, this way or that."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Three sleepless nights I passed in sounding on, Through words and things, a dim and perilous way."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "O Reader! Had you in your mind Such stores as silent thought can bring, O gentle Reader! you would find A tale in everything."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "In that sweet mood when pleasant thoughts Bring sad thoughts to the mind."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Nor less I deem that there are Powers Which of themselves our minds impress; That we can feed this mind of ours In a wise passiveness."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Up! up! my friend, and quit your books, Or surely you 'll grow double! Up! up! my friend, and clear your looks! Why all this toil and trouble?"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Come forth into the light of things, Let Nature be your teacher."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "One impulse from a vernal wood May teach you more of man, Of moral evil and of good, Than all the sages can."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "That best portion of a good man's life,-- His little, nameless, unremembered acts Of kindness and of love."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lines composed a few miles above Tintern Abbey

quote: "The fretful stir Unprofitable, and the fever of the world Have hung upon the beatings of my heart."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lines composed a few miles above Tintern Abbey

quote: "Knowing that Nature never did betray The heart that loved her."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lines composed a few miles above Tintern Abbey

quote: "Nor greetings where no kindness is, nor all The dreary intercourse of daily life."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lines composed a few miles above Tintern Abbey

quote: "Men who can hear the Decalogue, and feel No self-reproach."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "There 's something in a flying horse, There 's something in a huge balloon."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The common growth of Mother Earth Suffices me,--her tears, her mirth, Her humblest mirth and tears."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Full twenty times was Peter feared, For once that Peter was respected."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A primrose by a river's brim A yellow primrose was to him, And it was nothing more."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The soft blue sky did never melt Into his heart; he never felt The witchery of the soft blue sky!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "She dwelt among the untrodden ways Beside the springs of Dove,-- A maid whom there were none to praise And very few to love."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: She dwelt among the untrodden ways

quote: "A violet by a mossy stone Half hidden from the eye; Fair as a star, when only one Is shining in the sky."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: She dwelt among the untrodden ways

quote: "May no rude hand deface it, And its forlorn _hic jacet!_"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "She gave me eyes, she gave me ears; And humble cares, and delicate fears; A heart, the fountain of sweet tears; And love and thought and joy."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The child is father of the man."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: My heart leaps up when I behold

quote: "The cattle are grazing, Their heads never raising; There are forty feeding like one!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Sweet childish days, that were as long As twenty days are now."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: To a Butterfly. I 've watched you now a full half-hour

quote: "Often have I sighed to measure By myself a lonely pleasure,-- Sighed to think I read a book, Only read, perhaps, by me."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Choice word and measured phrase above the reach Of ordinary men."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Men are we, and must grieve when even the shade Of that which once was great is passed away."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: On the Extinction of the Venetian Republic

quote: "He murmurs near the running brooks A music sweeter than their own."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "And you must love him, ere to you He will seem worthy of your love."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The harvest of a quiet eye, That broods and sleeps on his own heart."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Yet sometimes, when the secret cup Of still and serious thought went round, It seemed as if he drank it up, He felt with spirit so profound."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "My eyes are dim with childish tears, My heart is idly stirred, For the same sound is in my ears Which in those days I heard."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A happy youth, and their old age Is beautiful and free."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The sweetest thing that ever grew Beside a human door."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Until a man might travel twelve stout miles, Or reap an acre of his neighbor's corn."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Something between a hindrance and a help."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Lady of the Mere, Sole-sitting by the shores of old romance."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: A narrow Girdle of rough Stones and Crags

quote: "And he is oft the wisest man Who is not wise at all."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A jolly place," said he, "in times of old! But something ails it now: the spot is cursed."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Hunt half a day for a forgotten dream."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Never to blend our pleasure or our pride With sorrow of the meanest thing that feels."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A noticeable man, with large gray eyes."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Stanzas written in Thomson's Castle of Indolence

quote: "Oft on the dappled turf at ease I sit, and play with similes, Loose type of things through all degrees."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Some natural sorrow, loss, or pain That has been, and may be again."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The music in my heart I bore Long after it was heard no more."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Let beeves and home-bred kine partake The sweets of Burn-mill meadow; The swan on still St. Mary's Lake Float double, swan and shadow!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Every gift of noble origin Is breathed upon by Hope's perpetual breath."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A creature not too bright or good For human nature's daily food; For transient sorrows, simple wiles, Praise, blame, love, kisses, tears, and smiles."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: She was a Phantom of Delight

quote: "The reason firm, the temperate will, Endurance, foresight, strength, and skill; A perfect woman, nobly planned, To warn, to comfort, and command."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: She was a Phantom of Delight

quote: "That inward eye Which is the bliss of solitude."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A light to guide, a rod To check the erring, and reprove."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The light that never was, on sea or land; The consecration, and the Poet's dream."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Suggested by a Picture of Peele Castle in a Storm. Stanza 4

quote: "Where the statue stood Of Newton, with his prism and silent face, The marble index of a mind forever Voyaging through strange seas of thought alone."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The budding rose above the rose full blown."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Controls them and subdues, transmutes, bereaves Of their bad influence, and their good receives."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "And through the heat of conflict keeps the law In calmness made, and sees what he foresaw."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Whom neither shape of danger can dismay, Nor thought of tender happiness betray."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The world is too much with us; late and soon, Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers: Little we see in Nature that is ours."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Sweetest melodies Are those that are by distance made more sweet."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A power is passing from the earth."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lines on the expected Dissolution of Mr. Fox

quote: "The rainbow comes and goes, And lovely is the rose."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Where is it now, the glory and the dream?"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Though nothing can bring back the hour Of splendour in the grass, of glory in the flower."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "In years that bring the philosophic mind."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "To me the meanest flower that blows can give Thoughts that do often lie too deep for tears."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Two voices are there: one is of the sea, One of the mountains,--each a mighty voice."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Thought of a Briton on the Subjugation of Switzerland

quote: "The silence that is in the starry sky."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Song at the Feast of Broughton Castle

quote: "A few strong instincts, and a few plain rules."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Alas! what boots the long laborious Quest?

quote: "This dull product of a scoffer's pen."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Wisdom is ofttimes nearer when we stoop Than when we soar."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The intellectual power, through words and things, Went sounding on a dim and perilous way!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Society became my glittering bride, And airy hopes my children."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "There is a luxury in self-dispraise; And inward self-disparagement affords To meditative spleen a grateful feast."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "So build we up the being that we are."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "One in whom persuasion and belief Had ripened into faith, and faith become A passionate intuition."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "A man he seems of cheerful yesterdays And confident to-morrows."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "The primal duties shine aloft, like stars; The charities that soothe and heal and bless Are scattered at the feet of man like flowers."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "By happy chance we saw A twofold image: on a grassy bank A snow-white ram, and in the crystal flood Another and the same!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Elysian beauty, melancholy grace, Brought from a pensive though a happy place."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Sad fancies do we then affect, In luxury of disrespect To our own prodigal excess Of too familiar happiness."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "That kill the bloom before its time, And blanch, without the owner's crime, The most resplendent hair."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Lament of Mary Queen of Scots

quote: "Meek Nature's evening comment on the shows That for oblivion take their daily birth From all the fuming vanities of earth."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Sky-Prospect from the Plain of France

quote: "Turning, for them who pass, the common dust Of servile opportunity to gold."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "To the solid ground Of Nature trusts the mind that builds for aye."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: A Volant Tribe of Bards on Earth

quote: "Soft is the music that would charm forever; The flower of sweetest smell is shy and lowly."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "True beauty dwells in deep retreats, Whose veil is unremoved Till heart with heart in concord beats, And the lover is beloved."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: To ----. Let other Bards of Angels sing

quote: "A Briton even in love should be A subject, not a slave!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Ere with Cold Beads of Midnight Dew

quote: "Scorn not the sonnet. Critic, you have frowned, Mindless of its just honours; with this key Shakespeare unlocked his heart."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "But he is risen, a later star of dawn."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Bright gem instinct with music, vocal spark."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "When his veering gait And every motion of his starry train Seem governed by a strain Of music, audible to him alone."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Stern Winter loves a dirge-like sound."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: On the Power of Sound. xii

quote: "The bosom-weight, your stubborn gift, That no philosophy can lift."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "How fast has brother followed brother, From sunshine to the sunless land!"
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Extempore Effusion upon the Death of James Hogg

quote: "Those old credulities, to Nature dear, Shall they no longer bloom upon the stock Of history?"
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: Memorials of a Tour in Italy. iv

quote: "How does the meadow-flower its bloom unfold? Because the lovely little flower is free Down to its root, and in that freedom bold."
author: Joseph Hopkinson
source: A Poet! He hath put his Heart to School

quote: "Minds that have nothing to confer Find little to perceive."
author: Joseph Hopkinson

quote: "Such is the custom of Branksome Hall."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lay of the Last Minstrel. Canto i. Stanza 7

quote: "I was not always a man of woe."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lay of the Last Minstrel. Canto ii. Stanza 12

quote: "I cannot tell how the truth may be; I say the tale as 't was said to me."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lay of the Last Minstrel. Canto ii. Stanza 22

quote: "Her blue eyes sought the west afar, For lovers love the western star."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lay of the Last Minstrel. Canto iii. Stanza 24

quote: "O Caledonia! stern and wild, Meet nurse for a poetic child! Land of brown heath and shaggy wood; Land of the mountain and the flood!"
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lay of the Last Minstrel. Canto vi. Stanza 2

quote: "When, musing on companions gone, We doubly feel ourselves alone."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Where 's the coward that would not dare To fight for such a land?"
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Lightly from fair to fair he flew, And loved to plead, lament, and sue; Suit lightly won, and short-lived pain, For monarchs seldom sigh in vain."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "With a smile on her lips and a tear in her eye."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Oh what a tangled web we weave, When first we practise to deceive!"
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Charge, Chester, charge! on, Stanley, on!" Were the last words of Marmion."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Oh for a blast of that dread horn On Fontarabian echoes borne!"
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "To all, to each, a fair good-night, And pleasing dreams, and slumbers light."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "In listening mood she seemed to stand, The guardian Naiad of the strand."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lady of the Lake. Canto i. Stanza 17

quote: "Sleep the sleep that knows not breaking, Morn of toil nor night of waking."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lady of the Lake. Canto i. Stanza 31

quote: "Hail to the chief who in triumph advances!"
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lady of the Lake. Canto ii. Stanza 19

quote: "Come one, come all! this rock shall fly From its firm base as soon as I."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lady of the Lake. Canto v. Stanza 10

quote: "Where, where was Roderick then? One blast upon his bugle horn Were worth a thousand men."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lady of the Lake. Canto vi. Stanza 18

quote: "Spangling the wave with lights as vain As pleasures in the vale of pain, That dazzle as they fade."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Lord of the Isles. Canto i. Stanza 23

quote: "Still are the thoughts to memory dear."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "A mother's pride, a father's joy."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Oh, Brignall banks are wild and fair, And Greta woods are green, And you may gather garlands there Would grace a summer's queen."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "No pale gradations quench his ray, No twilight dews his wrath allay."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Come as the winds come, when Forests are rended; Come as the waves come, when Navies are stranded."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "A lawyer without history or literature is a mechanic, a mere working mason; if he possesses some knowledge of these, he may venture to call himself an architect."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "There 's a gude time coming."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "My foot is on my native heath, and my name is MacGregor."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Scared out of his seven senses."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Sound, sound the clarion, fill the fife! To all the sensual world proclaim, One crowded hour of glorious life Is worth an age without a name."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Within that awful volume lies The mystery of mysteries!"
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Ah, County Guy, the hour is nigh, The sun has left the lea. The orange flower perfumes the bower, The breeze is on the sea."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Woman's faith and woman's trust, Write the characters in dust."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "I am she, O most bucolical juvenal, under whose charge are placed the milky mothers of the herd."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "But with the morning cool reflection came."
author: Sir Walter Scott
source: Chronicles of the Canongate. Chap. iv

quote: "What can they see in the longest kingly line in Europe, save that it runs back to a successful soldier?"
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "The playbill, which is said to have announced the tragedy of Hamlet, the character of the Prince of Denmark being left out."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Rouse the lion from his lair."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "Although too much of a soldier among sovereigns, no one could claim with better right to be a sovereign among soldiers."
author: Sir Walter Scott

quote: "When the good man yields his breath (For the good man never dies)."
author: James Montgomery
source: The Wanderer of Switzerland. Part v

quote: "Gashed with honourable scars, Low in Glory's lap they lie; Though they fell, they fell like stars, Streaming splendour through the sky."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Distinct as the billows, yet one as the sea."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Once, in the flight of ages past, There lived a man."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Counts his sure gains, and hurries back for more."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Joys too exquisite to last, And yet _more_ exquisite when past."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Bliss in possession will not last; Remembered joys are never past; At once the fountain, stream, and sea, They were, they are, they yet shall be."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "Beyond this vale of tears There is a life above, Unmeasured by the flight of years; And all that life is love."
author: James Montgomery
source: The Issues of Life and Death

quote: "Night is the time to weep, To wet with unseen tears Those graves of memory where sleep The joys of other years."
author: James Montgomery
source: The Issues of Life and Death

quote: "Here in the body pent, Absent from Him I roam, Yet nightly pitch my moving tent A day's march nearer home."
author: James Montgomery

quote: "He holds him with his glittering eye, And listens like a three years' child."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "We were the first that ever burst Into that silent sea."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "As idle as a painted ship Upon a painted ocean."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Water, water, everywhere, Nor any drop to drink."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Without a breeze, without a tide, She steadies with upright keel."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Alone, alone,--all, all alone; Alone on a wide, wide sea."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "The moving moon went up the sky, And nowhere did abide; Softly she was going up, And a star or two beside."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Oh sleep! it is a gentle thing, Beloved from pole to pole."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "A sadder and a wiser man, He rose the morrow morn."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "And the spring comes slowly up this way."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "A lady richly clad as she, Beautiful exceedingly."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Her gentle limbs did she undress, And lay down in her loveliness."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "A sight to dream of, not to tell!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "That saints will aid if men will call; For the blue sky bends over all!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Her face, oh call it fair, not pale!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "They stood aloof, the scars remaining,-- Like cliffs which had been rent asunder: A dreary sea now flows between."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "The grand old ballad of Sir Patrick Spence."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "A mother is a mother still, The holiest thing alive."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Joy rises in me, like a summer's morn."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "My eyes make pictures when they are shut."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "To know, to esteem, to love, and then to part, Makes up life's tale to many a feeling heart!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Flowers are lovely; love is flower-like; Friendship is a sheltering tree; Oh the joys that came down shower-like, Of friendship, love, and liberty, Ere I was old!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "I have heard of reasons manifold Why Love must needs be blind, But this the best of all I hold,-- His eyes are in his mind."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
source: To a Lady, Offended by a Sportive Observation

quote: "I counted two-and-seventy stenches, All well defined, and several stinks."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "I stood in unimaginable trance And agony that cannot be remembered."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Clothing the palpable and familiar With golden exhalations of the dawn."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
source: The Death of Wallenstein. Act i. Sc. 1

quote: "A dwarf sees farther than the giant when he has the giant's shoulder to mount on."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "That passage is what I call the sublime dashed to pieces by cutting too close with the fiery four-in-hand round the corner of nonsense."
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge

quote: "Iago's soliloquy, the motive-hunting of a motiveless malignity--how awful it is!"
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
source: Notes on some other Plays of Shakespeare

quote: "How does the water Come down at Lodore?"
author: Robert Southey

quote: "So I told them in rhyme, For of rhymes I had store."
author: Robert Southey

quote: "And this way the water comes down at Lodore."
author: Robert Southey

quote: "But what good came of it at last?" Quoth little Peterkin. "Why, that I cannot tell," said he; "But 't was a famous victory."
author: Robert Southey

quote: "What will not woman, gentle woman dare, When strong affection stirs her spirit up?"
author: Robert Southey

quote: "A clear fire, a clean hearth, and the rigour of the game."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "Sentimentally I am disposed to harmony; but organically I am incapable of a tune."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "Not if I know myself at all."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "It is good to love the unknown."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "Your absence of mind we have borne, till your presence of body came to be called in question by it."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "Gone before To that unknown and silent shore."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "I have had playmates, I have had companions, In my days of childhood, in my joyful school-days. All, all are gone, the old familiar faces."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "I like you and your book, ingenious Hone! In whose capacious all-embracing leaves The very marrow of tradition 's shown; And all that history, much that fiction weaves."
author: Charles Lamb
source: To the Editor of the Every-Day Book

quote: "He might have proved a useful adjunct, if not an ornament to society."
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "Martin, if dirt was trumps, what hands you would hold!"
author: Charles Lamb

quote: "I saw them go: one horse was blind, The tails of both hung down behind, Their shoes were on their feet."
author: James Smith

quote: "Lax in their gaiters, laxer in their gait."
author: James Smith

quote: "My eyes! what tiles and chimney-pots About their heads are flying!"
author: James Smith

quote: "And rival all but Shakespeare's name below."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Pleasures of Hope. Part i. Line 472

quote: "Without the smile from partial beauty won, Oh what were man?--a world without a sun."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Pleasures of Hope. Part ii. Line 21

quote: "There shall he love when genial morn appears, Like pensive Beauty smiling in her tears."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Pleasures of Hope. Part ii. Line 95

quote: "That gems the starry girdle of the year."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Pleasures of Hope. Part ii. Line 194

quote: "The hunter and the deer a shade."
author: Thomas Campbell

quote: "Whose lines are mottoes of the heart, Whose truths electrify the sage."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Ode to the Memory of Burns

quote: "The meteor flag of England Shall yet terrific burn, Till danger's troubled night depart, And the star of peace return."
author: Thomas Campbell

quote: "To bear is to conquer our fate."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: On visiting a Scene in Argyleshire

quote: "The sentinel stars set their watch in the sky."
author: Thomas Campbell

quote: "In life's morning march, when my bosom was young."
author: Thomas Campbell

quote: "A stoic of the woods,--a man without a tear."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Gertrude of Wyoming. Part i. Stanza 23

quote: "O Love! in such a wilderness as this."
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Gertrude of Wyoming. Part iii. Stanza 1

quote: "The torrent's smoothness, ere it dash below!"
author: Thomas Campbell
source: Gertrude of Wyoming. Part iii. Stanza 5

quote: "Oh leave this barren spot to me! Spare, woodman, spare the beechen tree!"
author: Thomas Campbell

quote: "The gentleman [Josiah Quincy] cannot have forgotten his own sentiment, uttered even on the floor of this House, "Peaceably if we can, forcibly if we must."
author: Henry Clay

quote: "Government is a trust, and the officers of the government are trustees; and both the trust and the trustees are created for the benefit of the people."
author: Henry Clay

quote: "I have heard something said about allegiance to the South. I know no South, no North, no East, no West, to which I owe any allegiance."
author: Henry Clay

quote: "Thinking is but an idle waste of thought, And nought is everything and everything is nought."
author: Horace Smith

quote: "In the name of the Prophet--figs."
author: Horace Smith

quote: "When Time who steals our years away Shall steal our pleasures too, The mem'ry of the past will stay, And half our joys renew."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Faintly as tolls the evening chime, Our voices keep tune and our oars keep time."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Row, brothers, row, the stream runs fast, The rapids are near, and the daylight 's past."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "The minds of some of our statesmen, like the pupil of the human eye, contract themselves the more, the stronger light there is shed upon them."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Who ran Through each mode of the lyre, and was master of all."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "When did morning ever break, And find such beaming eyes awake?"
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Rich and rare were the gems she wore, And a bright gold ring on her wand she bore."
author: Thomas Moore
source: Rich and rare were the Gems she wore

quote: "There is not in the wide world a valley so sweet As that vale in whose bosom the bright waters meet."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Shall I ask the brave soldier who fights by my side In the cause of mankind, if our creeds agree?"
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "The moon looks On many brooks "The brook can see no moon but this."
author: Thomas Moore
source: While gazing on the Moon's Light

quote: "And when once the young heart of a maiden is stolen, The maiden herself will steal after it soon."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "And the best of all ways To lengthen our days Is to steal a few hours from the night, my dear."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "You may break, you may shatter the vase if you will, But the scent of the roses will hang round it still."
author: Thomas Moore
source: Farewell! But whenever you welcome the Hour

quote: "The light that lies In woman's eyes."
author: Thomas Moore
source: The Time I 've lost in wooing

quote: "My only books Were woman's looks,-- And folly 's all they 've taught me."
author: Thomas Moore
source: The Time I 've lost in wooing

quote: "All that 's bright must fade,-- The brightest still the fleetest; All that 's sweet was made But to be lost when sweetest."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Those evening bells! those evening bells! How many a tale their music tells Of youth and home, and that sweet time When last I heard their soothing chime!"
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "A friendship that like love is warm; A love like friendship, steady."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "Oh call it by some better name, For friendship sounds too cold."
author: Thomas Moore
source: Oh call it by some better Name

quote: "To Greece we give our shining blades."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "When thus the heart is in a vein Of tender thought, the simplest strain Can touch it with peculiar power."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "To sigh, yet feel no pain; To weep, yet scarce know why; To sport an hour with Beauty's chain, Then throw it idly by."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "There 's a bower of roses by Bendemeer's stream."
author: Thomas Moore
source: Lalla Rookh. The Veiled Prophet of Khorassan

quote: "One morn a Peri at the gate Of Eden stood disconsolate."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "But the trail of the serpent is over them all."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "As sunshine broken in the rill, Though turned astray, is sunshine still."
author: Thomas Moore

quote: "A delusion, a mockery, and a snare."
author: Lord Denman
source: O'Connell v. The Queen, 11 Clark and Finnelly Reports

quote: "The scene was more beautiful far to the eye Than if day in its pride had arrayed it."
author: Paul Moon James

quote: "Knowledge is the only fountain both of the love and the principles of human liberty."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Completion of Bunker Hill Monument, June 17, 1843. P. 93

quote: "Washington is in the clear upper sky."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Eulogy on Adams and Jefferson, Aug. 2, 1826. Vol. i. p. 148

quote: "When tillage begins, other arts follow. The farmers therefore are the founders of human civilization."
author: Daniel Webster

quote: "Justice, sir, is the great interest of man on earth."
author: Daniel Webster

quote: "Liberty exists in proportion to wholesome restraint."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Speech at the Charleston Bar Dinner, May 10, 1847. Vol. ii. p. 393

quote: "The law: It has honored us; may we honor it."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Toast at the Charleston Bar Dinner, May 10, 1847. Vol. ii. p. 394

quote: "Labour in this country is independent and proud. It has not to ask the patronage of capital, but capital solicits the aid of labor."
author: Daniel Webster

quote: "The gentleman has not seen how to reply to this, otherwise than by supposing me to have advanced the doctrine that a national debt is a national blessing."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Second Speech on Foot's Resolution, Jan. 26, 1830. P. 303

quote: "The people's government, made for the people, made by the people, and answerable to the people."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Second Speech on Foot's Resolution, Jan. 26, 1830. P. 321

quote: "Liberty and Union, now and forever, one and inseparable."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Second Speech on Foot's Resolution, Jan. 26, 1830. Vol. iii. p. 342

quote: "Inconsistencies of opinion, arising from changes of circumstances, are often justifiable."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Speech, July 25 and 27, 1846. Vol. v. p. 187

quote: "There is nothing so powerful as truth,--and often nothing so strange."
author: Daniel Webster
source: Argument on the Murder of Captain White. Vol. vi. p. 68

quote: "Who ran to help me when I fell, And would some pretty story tell, Or kiss the place to make it well? My mother."
author: Jane Taylor

quote: "Failed the bright promise of your early day."
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "No hammers fell, no ponderous axes rung; Like some tall palm the mystic fabric sprung. Majestic silence!"
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "By cool Siloam's shady rill How sweet the lily grows!"
author: Reginald Heber
source: First Sunday after Epiphany. No. ii

quote: "When Spring unlocks the flowers to paint the laughing soil."
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "From Greenland's icy mountains, From India's coral strand, Where Afric's sunny fountains Roll down their golden sand."
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "Though every prospect pleases, And only man is vile."
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "I see them on their winding way, About their ranks the moonbeams play."
author: Reginald Heber

quote: "Free-livers on a small scale, who are prodigal within the compass of a guinea."
author: Washington Irving

quote: "Abou Ben Adhem (may his tribe increase!) Awoke one night from a deep dream of peace."
author: Leigh Hunt

quote: "Write me as one who loves his fellow-men."
author: Leigh Hunt

quote: "Oh for a seat in some poetic nook, Just hid with trees and sparkling with a brook!"
author: Leigh Hunt

quote: "With spots of sunny openings, and with nooks To lie and read in, sloping into brooks."
author: Leigh Hunt

quote: "How dear to this heart are the scenes of my childhood, When fond recollection presents them to view."
author: Samuel Woodworth

quote: "Then soon with the emblem of truth overflowing, And dripping with coolness, it rose from the well."
author: Samuel Woodworth

quote: "The old oaken bucket, the iron-bound bucket, The moss-covered bucket, which hung in the well."
author: Samuel Woodworth

quote: "While the hollow oak our palace is, Our heritage the sea."
author: Allan Cunningham
source: A Wet Sheet and a Flowing Sea
